Payoff/value at expiration : Call: Long Position , Call: Short Position , Put: Long Position , Put: Short Position ], rows: ["$X Profits are then given by: - Long position : $\quad \text{payoff} - CV_T(premium)$ - Short position :$\quad \text{payoff} + CV_T(premium)$ --- Long Call ![Options payoff --- Short Call !Options payoff --- Long Put !Options payoff --- Short Put !Options payoff --- Option Strategies If the agent has an expectation about the price development of the underlying: - Bullish strategies : generate a profit when the underlying's price increases - Bearish strategies : generate a profit when the underlying's price decreases - Non-directional strategies : generate a profit depending on the underlying's actual volatility. But... _what are the reasons for trading in options rather than trading in the underlying directly?_ - Leverage effect : option values respond more than proportionately to changes in the underlying's value. - Tailoring risk exposure of investment : options can be less risky due to limitation of the downside risk. - Portfolio insurance : it can be insured by long on a put for that stock. --- Option strategies: Protective Put Consider an agent that: - invest in a stock with price $S_0$ - wants to insure against potential declines in the stock price $$\quad \implies $$long put on the stock (exercise price $X$ in $T$ with premium $P_0$) !Options payoff --- Option strategies: spreads (I) Consider an agent that wants to take advantage of higher future price of the underlying. He could buy the stock, buy a call or short a put, or as an alternative, construct a portfolio that allows to _reduce the risk_ at the cost of _giving up part of the profits: This is called Bull spread strategy : - 1 long call with strike $X_1$ and premium $C_{0,1}$ - 1 short call with strike $X_2 > X_1$ and premium $C_{0,2}$ !Options payoff --- Option strategies: Spreads (II) The opposite also applies, i.e. consider an agent that wants to take advantage of _lower_ future price of the underlying. Bear spread strategy - 1 short call with strike $X_1$ and premium $C_{0,1}$ - 1 long call with strike $X_2 > X_1$ and premium $C_{0,2}$ !Options payoff --- Option strategies: Straddle Consider an agent that expect _large move_ in the stock's price but he's uncertain about the direction. Straddle Strategy : - 1 long call with strike $X$ and premium $C_0$ - 1 long put with strike $X$ and premium $C_0$ Moreover, if he thinks: - a stronger bullish move is expected: $\implies$ buy 2 calls and 1 put ( long strap ) - a stronger bearish move is expected: $\implies$ buy 2 puts and 1 call ( long strip ) !Options payoff --- 2. Option valuation In this chapter we focus on European options , please refer to the above sections for formal definition. We also use the following notation: - $S_0$: price of the underlying asset at toime $t = 0$ - $C_0, P_0$: prices (premia) of the call and the put option at time $t = 0$. - $r_f$: rate of the risk-free asset. An Arbitrage Opportunity is defined as a financial strategy that yields a (sure) cash flow $A_0 \ge 0$ at time $0$ and a cash-flow $A_T \ge 0$ at time $T$ and there is at least one stat of the word where one inequality is strict. - $A_0 > 0, A_T \ge 0$ almost surely is an arbitrage - $A_0 \ge 0, A_T \ge 0$ almost surely with $\text{Prob}[A_T > 0]>0$ is an arbitrage In this chapter, we assume that the market is arbitrage-free _(the market clears out arbitrage opportunities)_. --- Price Bounds on call options (European, no dividend) - Upper Bound: $C_0 \le S_0$ _Proof:_ Consider two portfolios: - Portfolio $A$: $1$ call + cash $PV(X) - Price in $t = 0$: $ [C_0 + PV(X)] - Value in $T$: $X + \max [S_t. X, 0] = \max [S_t, X] - Portfolio $B$: $1$ stock $S_0$ - Price in $t = 0: S_0$ - Value in $T$: $S_T$ Portfolio $A$ is never less valuable than portfolio in $T$, so $A$ cannot be less expensive in $t = 0$, otherwise we have an arbitrage opportunity, therefore we conclude: $$ C_0 + PV(X) \ge S_0 $$ and $C_0 \ge 0$ since the call is an option. - Lower Bound: $C_0 \ge \max [S_0 - PV(X), 0]$ where $PV$ is the present value operator. --- Price Bounds on put options (European, no dividend) - Upper bound: $P_0 \le PV(X)$ - Lower Bound: $P_0 \ge \max [PV(X) - S_0, 0]$ _Proof:_ Consider two portfolios: - Portfoio $C$: buy $1$ put + $1$ stock - Price in $t = 0$: $C_0 + S_0$ - Value in $T$: $\max(X - S_T, 0) + S_T = \max [S_t, X$] - Portfolio $D$: cash $PV(X)$ - Price in $t = 0$: $PV(X)$ - Value in $T$: $X$ $C$ is never less valuable than $D$, so $C$ cannot be less expensive in $t = 0$, otherwise we have an arbitrage opportunity, therefore: $$ P_0 + S_0 \ge PV(X) $$ and we add $P_0 \ge 0$ since the put is an option. --- The Put-Call Parity Theorem $$ S_0 + P_0 = \frac{X}{(1 + r_f)^T} + C_0 $$ To understand this, consider the following: - Portfolio $A$: $1$ stock and $1$ put - Portfolio $B$: $\frac{X}{(1 + r_f)^T}$ bonds and $1$ call the values of the two portfolio are equal in $t =T$, therefore they have to be equal also in $t = 0$, leading to the Put-Call parity formula. --- Option Valuation Determinants of the value _(price/premium)_ of a call option $C_0$ _(European, no dividend)_: - stock price $S_0$ (+), exercise price $K$ (-) - volatility of underlying stock (+) _(intuition: volatility increases the probability that the stock value at expiration is greater than $K$, increasing potential gains)_. - time to expiration $T-t$ (+) _(intuition: the more time to expiration, the higher the range od stock price increases, similar to volatility), also, the present value of exercise price falls)_ - interest rate (+) _(intuition: present value of exercise price falls)_. --- Two-state Option Valuation Model Consider a market with: - a stock that currently trades at $S_0$, and which value in $t = 1$ can be either $S_1^u, S_1^d$, with $S_1^u > S_1^d$ - call option on the stock with exercise price $X \in (S_1^d, S_1^u)$ and expiration date $T = 1$ - a risk free asset with yearly interest rate $r_f$ Then it is possible to replicate the payoffs of the call using a combination of the risk-free asset and the stock. The replicating strategy can be found as: $$ \begin{cases} \alpha S_1^u + \beta (1 + r_f) = S_1^u - X \\ \alpha S_1^d + \beta (1 + r_f)= 0 \end{cases} $$ which gives: $$ \alpha = \frac{S_1^u - X}{S_1^u - S_1^d}, \quad \beta = \frac{(S_1^u - X)S_1^d}{(1 + r_f)(S_1^d - S_1^u)} $$ In absence of arbitrage, since the price of the replicating strategy and the option must be the same $\forall t$, we can conclude that: $$C_0 = \alpha S_0 + \beta $$ _Intuition: in case of arbitrage, given the put-call parity formula $\text{C} + PV(K) = \text{P} + \text{S}$ we can construct an arbitrage opportunity by shorting the "cheapest" side and going long on the "expensive" one._ Note that the same reasoning _(with different derivations)_ can be applied to any payoff. We can now apply the same reasoning in continuous-time . Considering a given time range $T$, we split the time range in _(infinitely many)_ periods $dt$ and we assume that in any period the stock price is multiplied by either $(1 +\alpha dt), (1 + \beta dt)$. We assume the distribution of the stock price at time $T$ is log-normal . Applying _(infinitely many times)_ the two state valuation model makes it possible to compute the value of the call. --- Black-Scholes Formula Formally, the value of the stock at time $t$ denoted $S(t)$ is assume to vary as follows: $$ dS(t) = \mu S(dt) + \sigma S(t) dB(t) $$ where $B(t)$ is a standard Brownian Motion , so for any $y$ the distribution of $S(t)$ is log-normal. We then assume: - constant risk-free interest rate - no arbitrage and we get the famous Black-Scholes formula: $$ C_0 = S_0 N(d_1) - X e^{-rT} N (d_2) $$ - $d_1 = \frac{\ln (S_0 / X) + (r + \sigma^2/2)T}{\sigma \sqrt(T)} $ - $d_2 = d_1- \sigma \sqrt(T)$ - $N(d)$ probability that a random draw from a standard normal distribution will be less than $d$ - $r$: risk free interest rate, _(annualized continuously compounded rate with same maturity as the expiration of the option, note that is different from $r_f$)_, - $\sigma$: standard deviation of the annualized continuously compounded rate of return of the stock. _Intuition: $N(d)$ can be interpreted as the risk-adjusted probabilities that the call option will expire in-the-money ($X The Trillion Dollar Equation [VIDEO - Veritasium] --- 3. Pricing by arbitrage We consider two periods: _ex-ante, ex-post_, denoted by $t = 0,1$ and $\Omega$ contingent states at time $t = 1: \omega \in \{1, \ldots, \Omega\}$. There are $K$ assets available at time $t = 0: k \in {1, ... K}$. - $a_k^{\omega}$ is the value of asset $k$ in state $\omega$ at time $t = 1$ - we denote $p_k$ the price of asset $k$ at time $t = 0$ - a market is the data: $(a_k^{\omega}, p_k)$ A portfolio $(z_k)$ is a vector of asset quantities: $z_k$ is the quantit of asset $k$ in the portfolio. - the price of portolio is: $\sum_k p_k z_k$ - the value of the portfolio in state $\omega$ is: $\sum_k a_k^{\omega} z_k$ Given now a market with: $$ a = \begin{pmatrix} a_1^1 & \dots & a_1^\Omega \\ \vdots & & \vdots \\ a_K^1 & \dots & a_K^\Omega \end{pmatrix} \text{ and prices } p = \begin{pmatrix} p_1 \\ \vdots \\ p_K \end{pmatrix} $$ a portfolio with asset quantities $(z_1, ... z_k)$ has price: $$ (z_1, \dots, z_K) \begin{pmatrix} p_1 \\ \vdots \\ p_K \end{pmatrix} $$ and values $$ (z_1, \dots, z_K) \begin{pmatrix} a_1^1 & \dots & a_1^\Omega \\ \vdots & & \vdots \\ a_K^1 & \dots & a_K^\Omega \end{pmatrix}. $$ We then introduce the followig definitions: - an asset is risk-free if $a_k^{\omega}$ is independent of $\omega$ - an asset $k$ can be replicated by a subset of assets, indexed $i \in S$, if there exists a constant $(z_i)$ such that: $$ a_k^{\omega} = \sum_{i \in S, i \neq k} z_i a_i^{w} \quad \forall w $$ _(in such cas the asset $k$ is called redudant )_. A market $(a_k^{\omega}, p_k)$ is complete if for any asset $b^{\omega}$ there exists a constant $(z_k)$ such that: $$ b^{\omega} = \sum_k z_k a_k^{\omega} $$ In other words, the market is complete if the payoff matri has max rank: $$ \text{Complete} \iff \text{Rank}(A) = n \iff \text{A} \neq 0 $$ where $n$ is the number of states. On the other hand, markets are complete when all revenue configurations are replicable through some portfolio. Consequences of complete markets : - there are at least as many assets at the states: $| \Omega| \le K$ - a market is complete if and only if the payoff matrix $a$ ith dimension $(K \times \Omega)$ has rank $\Omega$. - if a market is complete with $K > \Omega$, there are $\Omega$ independent assets, then we can eliminate $K - \Omega$ assets which are linear combinations of the independent ones. --- An arbitrage portfolio is a portfolio $(z_k)$ such that: $$ \sum_k a_k^{w} z_k \ge 0\quad \forall \omega \\ \sum_k p_k z_k \le 0 $$ at least one of these $\Omega + 1$ inequalities being strict. A market is arbitrage free if there is no arbitrage portfolio. --- Law of One Price A market $(a_k^{w}, p_k)$ without arbitrage opportunity satisfies the Law of One Price: _If two assets $i,j$ satisfy $a_i^{\omega} = a_j^{\omega}$ for every state $\omega$, then the two assets have the same price $p_i = p_j$._ More on the law of one price [Investopedia] . Proof Assume that $p_i > p_j$, then the portfolio such that $z_i = -1, z_j = 1, z_k = 0$ for $k \neq i,j$ satisfies the following: $$ \sum_k a_k^{w}z_k = 0\\ \sum_k p_k z_k = p_j - p_i The No-Arbitrage Theorem A market $(a_k^{w}, p_k)$ is arbitrage-free is and only if there exists a vector $q = (q_1 ... q_{\Omega}) \in \R^{\Omega}$ such that: - $q^{\omega} > 0$ for every state - $p_k = \sum_{\omega} a_k^{\omega} q_{\omega}$ for every assets $q$ is then called state-price vector . In matrix form, the state-price vector with positive components solves: $$ \begin{pmatrix} p_1 \\ .\\.\\ p_k\end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} a_1^1 \quad ... \quad a_1^{\Omega}\\.\\.\\ a_K^1 \quad ... \quad a_K^{\Omega}\end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} q_1 \\.\\.\\ q_{\Omega}\end{pmatrix} $$ _There may exists several state price vectors in a market_. _Intuition_: - the state price ($q_\omega$) is simply the price you must pay today to receive that $1$ in state $\omega$ tomorrow _(regardless of the asset)_. - the state-price vector is just the collection of these prices for every possible future state. - $q$ doesnt tell you which assets to buy to receive $1$ - once you have the state-price vector $q$, you can price any asset $k$ using the sum above _(the price of an asset $k$ equals the sum of its payoff in each state weighted by the state prices)_ --- Risk Neutral Probability Assume a state price vector $(q_{\omega})$. Denote $\pi^*_{\omega} = \frac{q_w}{\sum_{\omega} q_{\omega}} > 0$, with $\sum_{\omega} \pi^*_{\omega} = 1$. Therefore $\pi^*_{\omega}$ can be interpreted as a probability. In particular, $(\pi^*_{\omega})$ is called the risk neutral probability. - if a market admits several state price vectors, it admits also as many risk neutral probabilities. If we define: $r_k^{\omega} = \frac{a_k^{\omega}}{p_k}$ the return of asset $k$ in state $\omega$, using $p_k = \sum_{\omega} a_k^{\omega}q_{\omega}$: $$ E^*[r_{k\omega}] = \sum_{\omega} \pi_\omega^* r_{k\omega} $$ If we substitute the definition of $\pi_\omega^* = \frac{q_\omega}{\sum_j q_j}$: $$ E^*[r_{k\omega}] = \sum_{\omega} \left( \frac{q_\omega}{\sum_j q_j} \right) \frac{a_{k\omega}}{p_k} $$ If we extract the constants ($p_k$ and the total sum of state prices) out of the summation: $$ E^*[r_{k\omega}] = \frac{1}{p_k \sum_{\omega} q_{\omega}} \sum_{\omega} q_\omega a_{k\omega} $$ Since $\sum_{\omega} q_\omega a_{k\omega}$ is exactly the definition of $p_k$, the terms cancel out: $$ E^*[r_{k\omega}] = \frac{1}{\sum_{\omega} q_{\omega}} $$ $$ \sum_{\omega} \pi^*_{\omega} r_k^{\omega} = \mathbb{E}^*_{\omega} [r_k^{\omega}] = \frac{1}{\sum_{\omega}q_{\omega}} $$ All asset returns have the same-risk neutral expectation. _(This does not imply that all assets have the same returns expectations according to physical probability)_. --- For any state $\omega_0$, the corresponding Arrow-Debreu security, denoted by $a_{\omega_0}^{\omega}$, is the asset with values given, for all $\omega$, by: $$ a_{\omega_0}^{\omega} = 1 _{\omega = \omega_0} $$ - If the market is arbitrage free and $q$ is a state-price vector, then the price of $a_{\omega_0}$ is $q_{\omega_0}$. - A market that contains all Arrow-Debreu securities is complete. _Intuition: a contract that pays exactly $1$ if a specific state of the world and $0$ in every other possible scenario._ Assume now that a market includes a risk-free asset that pays $1$ in all state of the world and denote by $p_0$ its price ($p_0 = \sum q_{\omega}$) and $r_f = 1 / p_0$, then: - for all assets $k$: $\mathbb{E}_{\pi^*}[r_k^{\omega}] = r_f$ - if the market contains the Arrow-Debreu security $a_{\omega_0}$, its price is $p_0 \pi^*_{\omega_0}$ --- Arbitrage Bounds Given an arbitrage-free market $M$, define $Q_M$ the set of state price vectors for this market: - $Q_M$ is a convex set - $Q_M$ empty $\iff M$ offers arbitrage opportunities - $Q_M$ is reduced to a point $\iff$ M is complete and arbitrage-free - if $q, q' \in Q_M$, then $q - q' \perp M$, that means: $$ \sum_{\omega} (q_{\omega} - q'_{\omega}) a_k^{\omega} = 0 \quad \forall k $$ --- Theorem (Arbitrage Bounds) For amy claim $c = (c_1, ... c_{\Omega})$, _not necessarily replicable_ in $M$, the set of prices $p_c$ for such a claim to not generage arbitrage opportunities is: $$ \{ p_c \quad|\quad p_c = \sum_{\omega} q_{\omega} c_{\omega} \text{ for some } q \in Q_M \} $$ - this set is singleton $\iff$ $c$ is replicable in $M$ - if $c$ is not replicable, the adding it to the market with a price within arbitrage bounds will reduce $Q_M$ _(dimension decreases by one)_ --- 4. An introduction to the economic analysis of asset markets Some introductionary definitions for the following chapters: We begin this chapter with a simple example about market equilibrium. Consider two agents $A,B$ and two-states of the world (ex-post) $w = 1,2$: - in state $ w = 1$, agents get income $w_A^1, w_B^1$ - in state $ w = 2$, agents get income $w_A^2, w_B^2$ Denote then by $c_A^1, c_A^2, c_B^1, c_B^2$ their consumption. The feasible allocations must satisfy: $$ c_A^1 + c_B^1 = w_A^1 + w_B^1\\ c_A^2 + c_B^2 = w_A^2 + w_B^2 $$ The no-trade situation corresponds to $c_A^1 = w_A^1, c_B^1 = w_B^1$ and $c_A^2 = w_A^2, c_B^2 = w_B^2$. !Feasible allocations In this chapter, we rely on the standard specification for the Expected Utility Theory : $$ U_i(c_i^1,c_i^2) = \pi_1 u_i (c_i^1) + \pi_2 u_i (c_i^2) $$ where $\pi_1, \pi_2$ are the probability for scenario $w = 1,2$ and $u_i : \R_+ \to \R$ is the utility index function . !Preferences !Preferences An allocation is Pareto-optimal $\iff$ there is no alternative feasible allocation at which every individual in the economy is at least as well off and some individuals is strictly better off. _Graphically_, Pareto-optimal allocations are the ones where the indifference curves are tangent . !Preferences The contract curve (or core) is the part of the Pareto set for which both agents do at least as well as their initial endownets. On the core, both agents gain from trade and the outcome is Pareto-optimal. !Preferences Agents interact by trading assets on a market: - assets are purchased/sold _ex-ange_ (before uncertainty realizes) - payoffs are given ex-post - market equilibrium occurs when asset prices are such that individual strategies are _globally compatible_ (demand = supply). --- A Complete Market We consider two assets _(Arrow-Debreu Securities)_ in a two-states world: - A risky asset $1$: - it can be purchased/sold ex-ante at $p_1$ - ex-post it pays $1$ for $w = 1$ and zero otherwise - A risky asset $2$: - it can purchased/sold ex-ante at $p_2$ - ex-post it pays $2$ for $w = 2$ and zero otherwise. $z_A^1, z_B^1, z_A^2, z_B^2$denote the quantities of assets for agents $A,B$ _(they can be positive or negative depending on purchase/sell)_. Ex-ante, each agent has zero initial wealth and a budget constraint: $$ p_1 z_i^1 + p_2 z_i^2 = 0 $$ Ex-post, assets' pay-offs and income and realize and agent $i$ consumes $w_i^i + z_i^i$. Therefore, agent $i$ solves the following optimization problem: $$ \max_{z_i^1, z_i^2} U^i (w_i^1 + z_i^1, w_i^2+ z_i^2) \quad p_1 z_i^1 + p_2 z_i^2 = 0 $$ This problem generates the demand functions: $$ z_i^2(w_i^1, w_i^2, p_1, p_2), \quad z_i^2(w_i^1,w_i^2, p_1,p_2) $$ Market Clearing A market is balanced _(cleared)_ when demand equals supply for all assets ( Market Clearing Conditions ): $$ z_A^1 + z_B^1 = 0\\ z_A^2 + z_B^2 = 0 $$ !No market clearing At equilibrium, we then have 2 equations in 2 unkwnowns: $$ z_A^1(w_A^1, w_A^2, p_1, p_2) + z_B^1(w_B^1, w_B^2, p_1, p_2) = 0\\ z_A^2(w_A^1, w_A^2, p_1, p_2) + z_B^2(w_B^1, w_B^2, p_1, p_2) = 0 $$ Considering also the budget constraints, the previous two equations are then equal, so we have $1$ independent equation and $2$ unknowns. $\implies$ Equilibrium prices are determined up to a multiplicative scalar . !No market clearing The following plot shows risk transfer: _A has NO income risk, but rather takes some risk from B_. !No market clearing Comonotonicity of Allocations The allocaitons $(c_A^1,c_A^2),(c_B^1,c_B^2)$ are comonotonic if and only if: $$ (c_A^1 - c_A^2) (c_B^1 - c_B^2) \ge 0 $$ !No market clearing All market equilibrium allocations are comonotone, this is also the case for Pareto-optimal allocations. --- Price responseto changes in risk Consider now the case where $B$'s endowment in state $2$ decreases, so $p_1/p_2$ is smaller. !No market clearing In this case, an increase in the risk of $B$ leads $A$ to take more risk. !No market clearing !No market clearing In general, low risk averse agents take the most of the risk. An interactive simulation of risk preferences can be found here . --- 5. Choice under uncertainty Consider $Z$ a set of possible consequences. Let $\Omega = \{ 1, 2, |\Omega| \}$ the set of possible states of the world and $L(\Omega)$ the set of lotteries with consequences in $Z$. A lottery is a list of pairs $(x^{\omega}, \pi_{\omega})$ where $\pi_{\omega}$ gives the probability that $\omega$ will occur, and $x^{\omega}$ is the outcome. _Example: $((0,0.5); (1,0.5))$ is the lottery that pays $0,1$ with equal probabilities._ A compound lottery is a lottery whose prizes are themselves lotteries. Mixture operation Consider two lotteries $L, L'$. The compound lottery with $\lambda \in [0,1]$ corresponds to the lottery which consists in playing the first lottery with probability $\lambda$ and the second with probability $(1-\lambda)$. The resulting lottery is denoted by $(\lambda L + (1-\lambda)L')$. The operation + is called a mixture operation . _Example:_ $$ L = ((0,p);(1, 1-p))\\ L' = ((1,q); (2, 1-q))\\ \lambda L + (1-\lambda)L' = \Big( (0, \lambda p); (1; \lambda(1-p)+ (1-\lambda)q); (2, (1-\lambda)(1-q)) \Big) $$ !Mixture --- Preference Relation We want to define a theory of preference defined over $L(Z)$. A _relation of preferences_ $\ge$ on $L(Z)$ is a binary relation which is: 1. Complete - for any $L, L' \in L(Z)$, we have $L \ge L' $ or $L' \ge L$ 2. Transitive - $L_1 \ge L_2$ and $L_2 \ge L_3 \implies L_1 \ge L_3$ --- Axioms of Expected Utility Theory Von Neumann and Morgenstern suggested to assume the following about the relation of preference: - Independence : for all $L, L', L"$ and $\lambda \in (0,1)$: $$ L \ge L' \iff \lambda L + (1 - \lambda)L" \ge \lambda L' + (1 - \lambda)L" $$ - Continuity : if $L \ge L' \ge L"$, then there exists $\lambda \in [0,1]$ such that $L' \sim \lambda L + (1 - \lambda)L"$. _Graphically, the independence axiom:_ !Mixture --- A relation of preferences is represented by a utility function $V: L(Z) \to \R$ if and only if: $L \ge L' \iff V(L) \ge V(L')$. Theorem (Von Neumann-Morgenstern) If $\ge$ is a preference relation on $L(Z)$ that satisfies the previous axioms, then there exists $u : Z \to \R$ such that: $$ (x^{\omega}, \pi_{\omega}) \ge (\tilde x^{\omega}, \tilde \pi_{\omega}) \iff \sum_{\omega} \pi_{\omega} u (x^{\omega}) \ge \sum_{\omega} \tilde \pi_{\omega} u (\tilde x^{\omega}) $$ that can also be rewritten as: $$ \mathbb{E}_{\pi_{\omega}}[u(x^{\omega})] \ge \mathbb{E}_{\tilde \pi_{\omega}}[u(\tilde x^{\omega})] $$ In this case, we say that _the agent is said to have "VNM preferences"_. A little note on terminology: - $V: L(Z) \to \R$ is the utility function _(expected utility function)_. - $u: Z \to \R$ is just a function that appears when looking at the structure of $V$ _(utility index)_. Furthermore, for the rest of the chapters, we assume that$u$ is strictly increasing and twice continuously differentiable . --- Risk Aversion We denote the average of lottery $L$ by $\mathbb{E}_[x] = \sum_{\omega} \pi_{\omega}x^{\omega}$. We represent with $\delta_{\mathbb{E}_[x]}$ the degenerate lottery that gives the expected payoff of $L$ with probability one. An agent is said to be risk averse if and only if for all $L \in L(Z)$: $$ \delta_{\mathbb{E}_[x]} \ge L $$ An agent with VNM preferences is risk averse if and only if her utility index is concave: $u'\ge 0, u''\le 0$. To prove it, we consider the _Jensen Inequality_: if a function $u$ is concave, then we have: $$ \mathbb{E}_L[u(x)] \le u(\mathbb{E}_L[x]) $$ --- _Recall:_ for any lottery $L$ we can define the cumulative distribution function $F_L: \R \to [0,1]$ as: $$ F_L(x) = \mathbb{P}(L \le x) $$ Second-order Stochastic Dominance A lottery $L$ is said to second-order stochastically dominates $L' (\text{SOSD})$ if and only if: $\mathbb{E}_L[x] \ge \mathbb{E}_L'[x]$ and: $$ \int_{-\infty}^y F_L(x)dx \le \int_{-\infty}^yF_{L'}(x)dx \quad \forall x $$ _(Note that the first order stochastic dominance implies the second one)_ Clearest Explanation of Second-Order Stochastic Dominance! [VIDEO] Understanding First-Order Stochastic Dominance[VIDEO] . !SOSD !SOSD --- Mean Preserving Spreads Consider $L_1, L_2$ with the same mean and with density functions $f_1(x), f_2(x)$. $L_2$ is said to be a mean preserving spread of $L_1$ if there exists an interval $I$ such tat: $$ f_2(x) \ge f_1(x)\quad \forall x \notin I \\ f_2(x) \le f_1(x) \quad \forall x \in I $$ !Mean Preserving --- Increases in Risk There are different ways to define an _increase in risk_: - $L$ is riskier than $L'$ if $L' \text{ SOSD } L$ _($L'$ second order stochastically dominates $L$)_. - $L$ is riskier than $L'$ if $L$ is a _mean preserving_ spread of $L'$. - _Adding a white noise:_ $L$ is riskier than $L'$ if $L = L' + \epsilon$, where $\mathbb{E}[\epsilon] = 0$. _(Note that $L, \epsilon$ do not need to be independently distributed)_. An agent with VNM preferences dislikes increases in risk if and only if her utility index $u$ is concave. --- Quantifying Risk Aversion - Certainty Equivalent For a lottery $L$, its certainty equivalent is amount $e_L \in \R$ such that: $\delta_{e_L} \sim L$ or, in other terms: $$ u(e_L) = \mathbb{E}_L[u(x)] $$ _Intuition: $e_L$ is the amount of money for which the individual is indifferent between playing $L$ and a fixed amount of money_. - For a concave utility _(risk averse agent):_ $\mathbb{E}_L[u(x)] - e_L > 0$ - For a linear utility _(risk neutral agent):_ $\mathbb{E}_L[u(x)] = e_L$ Risk Premium For any lottery $L$ with payoff $X$, define the certainty equivalent $e_L$ as: $$u(e_L) = \mathbb{E}_L[u(X)]$$ The **risk premium** is then: $$\pi_L = \mathbb{E}_L[X] - e_L$$ For a VNM agent, this is equivalent to: $$u(\mathbb{E}_L[X]-\pi_L) = \mathbb{E}_L[u(X)]$$ - A risk neutral individual has zero risk premium - A _(strictly)_ risk-averse agent associates a positive risk premium to any _non-degenerate_ lottery. !Risk Premium _Intuition (preference rule): a VNM agent (risk-averse) always prefers the lottery with the higher certainty equivalent. If the lotteries have the same mean $\mathbb{E}[X]$, the agent prefers the one with the smaller risk premium_. Absolute Risk Premium for small risks Consider $x \in \R$, $\epsilon$ small and the following lottery: $$ L = x + \tilde \epsilon, \quad \mathbb{E}[\tilde \epsilon ] = 0 $$ Using Taylor expansions, we can show that the (absolute) risk premium $\pi_a$ when $\epsilon$ is small _(Arrow-Pratt approximation)_ is such: $$ \pi_a \approx -\frac{1}{2} \frac{u''(x)}{u'(x)} \mathbb{E}[\tilde \epsilon^2] $$ The absolute risk aversion coefficient $R_a(x)$ for wealth level $x$ is defined as: $$ R_a(x) = -\frac{u''(x)}{u'(x)} $$ For small risks of the form $x + \tilde \epsilon$: $$ \pi_a \approx \frac{1}{2}R_a (x)\mathbb{E}[\tilde \epsilon ^2] $$ _Note that $\mathbb{E}[\tilde \epsilon ^2]$ is the variance of the risk._ Relative Risk premium for small risks Consider $x \in \mathbb{R}$ and the same lottery as before, $L + \tilde \epsilon$. We can define the relative risk premium $\pi_r$ by: $$ (1 + \tilde \epsilon) x \sim (1 + \pi_r)x $$ and, using the Taylor expansion when $\tilde \epsilon$ is small: $$ \pi_r \approx - \frac{1}{2}\frac{x u''(x)}{u'(x)}\mathbb{E}[\tilde \epsilon ^2] $$ The relative risk aversion coefficient $R_r(x)$ for wealth level $x$ is then defined as: $$ R_r(x) = -\frac{x u''(x)}{u'(x)} = x R_a(x) $$ For a small risk of the form $(1 + \tilde \epsilon)x$: $$ \pi_r \approx \frac{1}{2}R_r(x)\mathbb{E}[\tilde \epsilon ^2] $$ --- Comparing Risk Aversion For two agents $A, B$ all the following sentences are equivalent: - $\forall L$ the certainty equivalent of $A$ is smaller or equal than the one of $B$. - $\forall L$ the risk premium for $B$ is smaller or equal than the one for $A$ - $u_A$ is more concave than $u_B$: there exists and increasing concave functio $\phi$ such that: $u_A = \phi (u_B)$. - $\forall x$ we have: $$ -\frac{u''_A(x)}{u'_A(x)} \ge - \frac{u''_B(x)}{u'_B(x)} $$ -$A$ is at least as risk averse as $B$ --- Common Classes of VNM utility indices - Costant Absolute Return Aversion (CARA) $$ u_{\lambda}(x) = \frac{1 - e^{-\lambda x}}{\lambda}, \quad u_0 = x $$ - Constant Relative Risk Aversion (CRRA) $$ u_{\gamma}(x) = \frac{x^{1-\gamma}}{1-\gamma},\quad \forall \gamma >0, \gamma \neq 1\quad u_1 = \log(x) $$ - Hyperbolic Absolute Risk Aversion (HARA) $$ u(x) = \frac{\gamma}{1 - \gamma}(b + x/\gamma)^{1-\gamma}, \quad b \ge 0 \implies R_a = \frac{\gamma}{x + b \gamma} $$ - Quadratic Utility Index $$ u(x) = x - \frac{\beta}{2}x^2, \quad R_a = \frac{\beta}{1 - \beta x} $$ Note that for a quadratic utility index: $$ \mathbb{E}[u(x)] = \mathbb{E}[x] - \frac{\beta}{2}\mathbb{E}[x^2] = \bar x - \frac{\beta}{2}\bar x^2 - \frac{\beta}{2}\text{Var}(x) $$ then the utility of a lottery only depends on its mean and variance. --- 6. Demand for risk Static Portfolio Choice - Setting Up the Problem Consider an agent with wealth $W$ who can invest at time zero $t_0$ in a risk-free asset with return $r_0$ and in a risky asset with random return $\tilde r$. If the agent invests $\alpha$ in the risky assets, he gets in period $1$: $$ (W - \alpha) r_0 + \alpha \tilde r = W r_0 + \alpha (\tilde r - r_0) $$ with $\omega = Wr_0$ and $ \tilde R = \tilde r - r_0$, the agent's problem rewrites as: $$ \max_{\alpha} V(\alpha) = \mathbb{E}[u(w + \alpha \tilde R)] $$ Positive Demand for Risky Asset Let's start deriving some properties: $$ V'(\alpha) = \mathbb{E}[\tilde R u'(w + \alpha \tilde R)] $$ $$ V''(\alpha) = \mathbb{E}[\tilde R^2 u''(w + \alpha \tilde R)] $$ Then if the agent is risk-averse, $V$ is concave and therefore $V'$ is _non-increasing_, so we can conclude that: - if $\mathbb{E}[\tilde r] > r_f \implies \mathbb{E}[\tilde R] > 0$: the agent should purchase some risky asset _(regardless of how much risk averse he migh be)_. - if $\mathbb{E}[\tilde r] Static Portfolio Choice - First Order Conditions We derive the first order conditions to quantify how much the agent should invest. $$ V'(\alpha^*) = 0 \implies \mathbb{E}[\tilde R u' (\omega \alpha^* \tilde R) r] = 0 $$ - If $\tilde R$ is always of the same sign $\implies$ there is _no interior solution_. - Even if the agent is risk-averse and $\tilde R$ changes sign, there _may not be_ an interior solution. In the following, we assume that there exists an interior solution and that $\mathbb{E}[\tilde R] > 0$, so that the solution is positive ($\alpha^* > 0$). Static Portfolio Choice - Small Risks We assume $\tilde R = \epsilon + \tilde R'$, with $\mathbb{E}[\tilde R'] =0$ and $\epsilon > 0$. We denote by $\alpha(\epsilon)$ the solution to: $$ \max_{\alpha} \mathbb{E}[ u(\omega (\epsilon + \tilde R') \alpha)] $$ from which we can derive the first-order condition : $$ \mathbb{E}[(\epsilon + \tilde R')u'(\omega + \alpha(\epsilon)\epsilon + \alpha (\epsilon)\tilde R')] = 0 $$ We know that $\alpha(0) = 0$, so using a Taylor Approximation, we expand $\alpha(\epsilon)$ around $\epsilon = 0$. Since $\alpha(0) = 0$ _(at zero mean excess return, the agent doesn't buy the risky asset),_ the first-order Taylor expansion is: $$\alpha(\epsilon) \approx \alpha(0) + \epsilon \alpha'(0) = \epsilon \alpha'(0)$$. We then substitute into the wealth argument, so the final wealth is: $$W(\epsilon) = \omega + \alpha(\epsilon)\epsilon + \alpha(\epsilon)\tilde R' = \omega + \alpha(\epsilon)(\epsilon + \tilde R')$$ Substituting $\alpha(\epsilon) \approx \epsilon \alpha'(0)$: $$W(\epsilon) \approx \omega + \epsilon \alpha'(0)(\epsilon + \tilde R') = \omega + \epsilon^2 \alpha'(0) + \epsilon \alpha'(0) \tilde R'$$ We then expand $u'(W(\epsilon))$ around $\omega$, using $u'(x) \approx u'(\omega) + (x - \omega)u''(\omega)$: $$u'(W(\epsilon)) \approx u'(\omega) + [W(\epsilon) - \omega] u''(\omega)$$ $$= u'(\omega) + [\epsilon^2 \alpha'(0) + \epsilon \alpha'(0) \tilde R'] u''(\omega)$$ The $\epsilon^2$ term is second-order in $\epsilon$, so we drop it: $$u'(W(\epsilon)) \approx u'(\omega) + \epsilon \alpha'(0) \tilde R' u''(\omega)$$ Since $\tilde R = \epsilon + \tilde R'$, for small $\epsilon$ we can write this as: $$u'(\omega + \alpha(\epsilon)\epsilon + \alpha(\epsilon)\tilde R') \approx u'(\omega) + \epsilon \alpha'(0) \tilde R u''(\omega)$$ If we plug it into the first-order condition, we obtain: $$ \epsilon u'(\omega) + \epsilon \alpha' (0) u''(\omega) \mathbb{E}[\tilde R'^2] = 0 $$ $$ \implies \alpha'(0) = \frac{1}{\mathbb{E}[\tilde R'^2] R_a (\omega)} $$ that finally gives: $$ \alpha \approx \frac{\mathbb{E}[\tilde R]}{\mathbb{V}(\tilde R) R_a (\omega)} $$ Then the share of wealth invested in the risky asset is: $$ \frac{\alpha}{\omega} \approx \frac{\mathbb{E}[\tilde R]}{\mathbb{V}(\tilde R) R_r (\omega)} $$ where $R_r(\omega) = - \omega \frac{u''(\omega)}{u'(\omega)}$ is the coefficient of relative risk aversion. --- Static Portfolio Choice - Quadratic Utility Indices We assume now quadratic utility index , with $c = \omega + \alpha + \tilde R$, that is: $$ u(c) = c - \frac{\beta}{2}c^2 $$ Under this scenario, the FOC writes as: $$ (1 - \beta \omega) \mathbb{E}[\tilde R] - \alpha \beta \mathbb{E}[\tilde R^2]= 0 $$ that brings the following value for $\alpha$: $$ \alpha = \frac{1- \beta \omega}{\beta}\frac{\mathbb{E}[\tilde R]}{\mathbb{E}[\tilde R^2]} $$ _Intuition: the amount invested in the risky asset only depends on the mean and the variance of $\tilde R = \tilde r - r_0$._ --- Static Portfolio Choice - Hyperbolic Absolute Risk Aversion Consider now the HARA utility index, with $c = \omega + \alpha \tilde R$. $$ u(c) = \frac{\gamma}{1 - \gamma} ( b + \frac{c}{\gamma})^{(1-\gamma)} $$ Under this scenario, the FOC gives: $$ \mathbb{E}[\tilde R (\omega + \alpha \tilde R + b \gamma)^{-\gamma}] = 0 \iff $$ $$ \iff \mathbb{E}[\tilde R (1 + \frac{\alpha}{(\omega / \gamma + b)\gamma})^{-\gamma}] = 0 $$ if we denote by $\alpha$ the solution of $ \mathbb{E}[\tilde R (1 + a \frac{\tilde R}{\gamma})^{-\gamma}] = 0$, we end up with: $$ a = \frac{\alpha}{(\omega/\gamma + b)\gamma} \quad \implies \quad \alpha = a \left(\frac{\omega}{\gamma}+b\right)\gamma $$ _Inutiotion behind corner cases:_ - CARA ($\gamma \to \infty$): $\alpha$ is independent of $\omega$. - CRRA ($b = 0$): $\alpha$ is proportional to $\omega$. --- Static Portfolio Choice - The Impact of Risk Aversion Proposition The demand for the risky asset decreases with risk aversion. Proof We consider $u_1 = \phi (u_2)$, with $\phi$ concave, then we assume that $\alpha_1, \alpha_2$ solve the following: $$ \mathbb{E}[\tilde R u_1 (\omega + \alpha_1 \tilde R)] = 0\quad \mathbb{E}[\tilde R u_2 (\omega + \alpha_2 \tilde R)] = 0 $$ that translates to $V'_1(\alpha_1) = V'_2(\alpha_2) = 0$. The concavity implies that: $$ \forall \alpha \in [0,1], \quad \forall R \in \mathbb{R}: $$ $$ R \phi' (u_2 (\omega + \alpha R)) \le R \phi0 (u_2 (\omega)) $$ Therefore we can write: $$ \begin{align*} V'_1(\alpha_2) &= \mathbb{E}[{\tilde R u'_1 (\omega + \alpha_2 \tilde R)}\\ &= \mathbb{E}[{\tilde R u'_2 (\omega + \alpha_2 \tilde R)} \phi' (u_2 (\omega + \alpha_2 \tilde R))]\\ &\le \mathbb{E}[\tilde R u'_2(\omega + \alpha_2 \tilde R)\phi'(u_2(\omega))]\\ &= \phi'(u_2 (\omega))V'_2 (\alpha_2)// &= 0 \end{align*} $$ Thus, $V'_1(\alpha_2)\le 0 = V'(\alpha_1)$. Since $V$ concave, we have also that $\alpha_1 \le \alpha_2$. --- Wealth and Demand for Risk A utility index is said to exhibit decreasing absolute risk aversion (DARA) if and only if $R_a$ is a decreasing function of wealth. Proposition $I$ The utility index $u$ exhinbits DARA if and only if for all $\tilde x$ the risk premium $\pi$ associated with the lottery $\omega + \tilde x$ is decraeasing in $\omega$. Consider now an expected utility maximiizng investor with utility index $u$ and initial wealth $\omega$. Assume that there is one safe asset and one risky asset with higher expected return. Proposition $II$ If the absolute risk aversion is decreasing _(increasing)_ then the dollar amount invested in the risky asset increases (decreases) with investor's wealth. Proposition $II$ If the relative risk aversion is increasing _(decreasing)_, then the fration of wealth invested in the risky asset decreases _(increases)_ with wealth. !Risky Asset _Example: share of wealth to risy asset over wealth percentiles (Fagerend, Andreas et al: "Heterogeneity and persistence in return to wealth")_. _Intuition:_ - it is common discussion that wealthier people are willing to bear more risk than poorer people - this is often complemented by a stronger assumption: _non-increasing (or decreasing) relative risk aversion_. Impact of a change in risk Should the demand for a risky asset increase or decrease with the riskiness of such an asset? Proposition A shift of the distribution of returns increases the demand for the risky asset is: - the shift is first-order stochastically dominant and $R_r(x) 7. Risk sharing and insurance We consider a single good and two dates: - $t_0$: where the contingent exchange contracts are signed - $t_1$: where the contracts are settled. We now consider a probability space, $\Omega$, with $\omega \in \Omega$ is a state of the world. $\pi_{\omega} \ge 0$ is the known probability distribution on $\Omega \implies$ if state of the world $\omega$ occurs, a total amount of $e^{\omega}$ resources is shared across agents. There is a infite number of agents. They have NVM preferences and are risk averse. The utility of agent $i$ is denoted as $u_i$ and satisfites the _Inada Condition_: $$ \lim_{c \to 0} u_i'(c) = + \infty $$ Optimal Allocation An allocation $c = (c_i^{\omega})_{1 \le i \le I}$ with $c_i^{\omega}\ge 0$ is the specification of a contingent consumption plan for all the individuals $i$ in each state of the world. An allocation is feasible if in each state the total consumption equals the total resources: $$ \forall \omega, \sum_{i = 1}^{I} c_i^{\omega} = e^{\omega} $$ An allocation is Pareto Optimal if it is a _feasible_ and if there is no other feasible allocation $\tilde c$ such that, $\forall i \in \{1,... I\}$: $$ \mathbb{E}_{\pi}[u_i(\tilde c_i)] \ge \mathbb{E}_{\pi}[u_i( c_i)] $$ with at least one strict inequality. --- Mutuality Principle Proposition (Borch) If all agents are strictly risk averse and have identical beliefs _(same information)_, then for any Pareto-optimal alllocation: $$ e^{\omega} = e^{\omega'} \implies c_i^{\omega} = c_i^{\omega'} $$ Let's then proceed with the following example. Consider the matrix of state-contingent consumption, where lines corresponds to individuals and column to states of the world: $$ c = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 5 & 4 & 8 & 9 \\ 2 & 5 & 3 & 7 & 8 \\ 4 & 6 & 5 & 12 & 10 \end{bmatrix} $$ In this case, the matrix is not a Pareto Optimal allocation. The columns represent the aggregate resources by state: $$ e = (8, 16, 12, 27, 27) $$ State 4 and 5 have the same aggregate resources: $e^4 = e^5 = 27$, but the Borch condition is not satisfied: $c^4 = (8, 7, 12) \neq c^5 = (9, 8, 10)$. --- Optimal allocation: Characterization Theorem A feasible allocation $c$ such that $c_i^{\omega} > 0$ is Pareto optimal $\iff$ there are nonnegative weights $(\lambda^i)_{1 \le i le I}$ and $(\mu^{\omega})_{\omega \in \Omega}$ such tat $\forall \omega \in \Omega$ and $\forall i$: $$ \lambda^i u_i'(c_i^{\omega}) = \mu^{\omega} $$ This condition can be restated as: - the ratio of marginal utilities in a given state $\omega$ for 2 agents $i \neq j$ is independent of the state $\omega$: $$ \frac{u'_i(c_i^{\omega})}{u_j'(c_j^{\omega})} = \frac{\lambda_j}{\lambda_i} $$ - the ratio of margina utilities in 2 given state $\omega, \omega'$ is the same across all agents: $$ \frac{u_i'(c_i^{\omega})}{u_i'(c_i^{\omega_i})} = \frac{\mu^{\omega}}{\mu^{\omega'}} $$ Corollary Assume that all agents are risk averse. Then, for any Pareto-optimal allocation, $c$ and $e$ are comonotonic: $$ e^{\omega} \ge e^{\omega'} \iff c_i^{\omega} \ge c_i^{\omega'} $$ _Intuition: $e$ is about the aggregate risk (how much total output/income the economy has in each scenario, $c$ is about _risk sharing_, who beras how much risk across scenarios)_. Since $\sum_{i = 1}^I c_i^{\omega} = e^{\omega}$, the variables $e^{\omega}, c_i^{\omega}$ are also comonotonic. Proof Assume that the allocation is Pareto optimal. Then $\lambda_i u_iì(c_i) = \mu^{\omega}$ and the ratio $u_i'(c_i^{\omega})/u_i'(c_i^{\omega'}) =\mu^{\omega}/\mu^{\omega'}$ is _independent_ of $i$. Hence $\forall i$ the cariables $c_i^{\omega}$ are _comonotonic_: $$ c_i^{\omega} > c_i^{\omega'} \iff c_j^{\omega} \ge c_j^¶{\omega'} $$ Let's proceed with an example. Consider the following matrix where lines correspond to individuals and columns to states of the world: $$ c = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 5 & 4 & 8 & 9\\ 2 & 5 & 4 & 8 & 11\\ 4 & 6 & 5 & 11 & 12 \end{bmatrix} $$ In this case, we have resources equal to: $$ e^{\omega} = \sum_i(c_i^{\omega}) = (8, 16, 13, 27, 32) $$ To check if it is Pareto-optimal for risk averse individuals _(not necessarily with the same preferences)_, we check the _commonoticity with aggregate endowment/resources_: when $e^{\omega}$ is higher, also each $c_i^{\omega}$ should be _weakly higher_. We order the states by $(e)$, $(1 Risk neutral agents should bear all the risk Corollary Assume that there is a risk-neutral agent and that the allocation $(c_i)_{1 \le i \le I}$ is pareto-optimal. If agent $i$ is strictly risk-averse, then her consumption must be determnistic: $$ \forall (\omega, \omega') \in \Omega^2, \quad c_i^{\omega} =

notes.title: Principles of Econometrics
notes.fullText: Index - Introduction to Statistics and Econometrics - Simple Regression Analysis - Multiple Regression Analysis: Basics - Multiple Regression Analysis: Inference - Multiple Regression Analysis: Further Issues - Heteroskedasticity and Other Problems - Time Series Data - Panel Data - Instrumental Variables 1 Introduction to Statistics and Econometrics Econometrics is the use of statistical methods to analyze economic data, starting _typically_ from non-experimental data . - estimating relationships between variables - testing economic theories and hypotheses - evaluating and implementing government and business policy Structure of Econometric Data - Cross sectional data - observations that represent individuals, firms, cantons, countries _(normally, but not necessarily, at one point in time)_. - observations are drawn randomly from a population (if not, we have a sample-selection problem) - Time Series Data - observations represent periods in time - observations are consecutive and hence not random - Pool Cross-Sections - at least two cross sections are combined in one data set - cross sections are drawn independently of each other - can often be treated similar to a normal cross section - Panel (or longitudinal data) - cross-sectional units followed over time - panel data have a cross-sectional and time series dimension - useful to account for time-invariant unobservables and for modeling lagged responses Causality The definition of _causal effect_ of $x$ on $Y$ is: _"how does variable $y$ change if variable $x$ is changed, but all other factors are held constant? _ (This concept is called Ceteris Paribus ). Simply establishing a relationship _(correlation)_ between variables can be misleading _(important distinction between correlation and causation!)_ There are multiple types of experiments that allow answering causal questions: - Randomized controlled trials (RCT) - Natural experiments 2 Simple Regression Analysis _How can we use the data to describe economic relations or behaviors?_ $$ y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x + \mu $$ - $y$: dependent variable _(outcome variable)_ - $x$: independent variable _(regressor, covariate, control variable, "the cause")_ - $\mu$: error term _(disturbance)_ - it represents strictly _unpredictable_ random behaviors, _unspecified_ or _unobserved_ factors or an _approximation error_ if the relation is not perfectly linear. - $\beta_0$: intercept parameter - $\beta_1$: slope parameter We start now with Population Modelling and consider the following assumptions: - SLR.1: Linear parameters - in the population model the following relation holds: $y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x + \mu$ -- SLR.2: Random sampling - we have a random sample of size $n$ of pairs $(x_i,y_i)$ - SLR.3: Sample variation in the explanatory variable - the sample outcomes on $x$ are not all the same value - SLR.4: Zero Conditional Mean $$ \mathbb{E}[\mu | x] = \mathbb{E}[\mu] = 0 $$ or, _in other words_, for every slice of the population determined by $x$, the average of $\mu$ is equal to the population average $\mu$ (which is zero). This also implies: $$ \mathbb{E}[y | x] = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x $$ also called the Population Regression Function . !Population Model We now derive the Ordinary Least Squares (OLS). _Intuition:_ we estimate the population parameters from a data sample of two random variables. - Let $\{(x_i, y_i) \} : \quad i = 1, \ldots, n$ denote a random sample of size $n$ - scatter plot in a $(x,y)$ system - the regression equation $y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_i + \mu_i$ allocates to each $x_i$ a value $y_i$ including a disturbance term $\mu_i$. But _how can we derive the estimates for_ $\beta_0, \beta_1$ We firstly rely on the SLR.4 assumption to derive two equations: $$ \begin{align*} \mathbb{E}[\mu] &= 0 \quad \implies \mathbb{E}[y - \beta_0 - \beta_1 x] = 0 \\ \mathbb{E}[u | x] &=0 \quad \implies \mathrm{Cov}[x,u] = \mathbb{E}[x \mu] - \mathbb{E}[x]\mathbb{E}[\mu] =\\ &= \mathbb{E}[x \mu] = \mathbb{E}[ x ( y - \beta_0 - \beta_1 x)] = 0 \end{align*} $$ where the second equation comes from the covariance formula and the fact that $\mathbb{E}[\mu] = 0$. These are called population moment restrictions Using then the sample moments from the population, we can get estimates of the population moments. Recall: - $\sum_i (y_i - \beta_0 - \beta_1 x_i)/n = 0\quad \quad (1)$ - $\sum_i x_i((y_i - \beta_0) - \beta_1 x_i)/n = 0 \quad (2)$ We rewrite $(1)$ as: $$ b_0 = \bar y - b_1 \bar x $$ Plugging this into $(2)$ and solving yields the familiar slope estimator: $$ b_1 = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i - \bar{x})^2}. $$ To obtain the intercept $b_0$, start from the sample first-order condition (1): $$ \sum_{i=1}^n (y_i - b_0 - b_1 x_i) = 0. $$ Rearrange: $$ n b_0 = \sum_{i=1}^n y_i - b_1 \sum_{i=1}^n x_i $$ Divide by $n$ and use the sample means $\bar y$ and $\bar x$: $$ b_0 = \bar y - b_1 \bar x. $$ Substituting the closed-form $b_1$ gives the explicit intercept estimate: $$ b_0 = \bar y - \bar x \; \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i - \bar{x})^2}. $$ This algebra shows the OLS line passes through the point $(\bar x,\bar y)$ and yields the intercept as the sample mean of $y$ minus the slope times the sample mean of $x$. _Intuition_: the slope estimate is the sample covariance between $x$ and $y$, divided by the sample variance of $x$. - if $x,y$ are positively correlated, the slope will be positive. - if $x,y$ are negatively correlated, the slope will be negative. _Moreover, intuitively_, the OLS is fitting a line through the sample points such that the sum of squared residuals is as small as possible . Algebraic Properties of OLS : - the sum of the OLS residuals is zero. - the sample average of the OLS residuals is zero. - the sample covariance between the regressors and the OLS residuals is zero. - the OLS regression line always goes through the mean of the sample. Moreover, we notice that _each observation $y_i$ is_ made up of an explained $\hat y_i$ and unexplained part $\hat u_i$ ($y_i = \hat y_i + \hat u_i$). Using this terminology, we can define: - $\text{Sum of Squares Total (SST)} = \sum_i (y_i - \bar y)^2$ - $\text{Sum of Squares Explained (SSE)} = \sum_i (\hat y_i - \bar y)^2$ - $\text{Sum of Squares Residuals (SSR)} = \sum_i (\hat u_i)^2$ To understand how well does the sample fit the regression line, we define: $$ R^2 = \frac{\text{SSE}}{\text{SST}} = 1 - \frac{\text{SSR}}{\text{SST}} $$ The second equality follows from the decomposition of total variation. Write each deviation from the mean as the explained part plus the residual: $y_i-\bar y=(\hat y_i-\bar y)+\hat u_i$. Then $$ \text{SST}=\sum_i (y_i-\bar y)^2=\sum_i\big[(\hat y_i-\bar y)+\hat u_i\big]^2 =\sum_i(\hat y_i-\bar y)^2+\sum_i\hat u_i^2+2\sum_i(\hat y_i-\bar y)\hat u_i. $$ The cross term vanishes because OLS residuals are orthogonal to the fitted values (equivalently to the regressors): $$ \sum_i(\hat y_i-\bar y)\hat u_i = 0. $$ Therefore $$ \text{SST}=\text{SSE}+\text{SSR}. $$ Dividing by $\text{SST}$ gives $$ 1=\frac{\text{SSE}}{\text{SST}}+\frac{\text{SSR}}{\text{SST}}\quad\Longrightarrow\quad \frac{\text{SSE}}{\text{SST}}=1-\frac{\text{SSR}}{\text{SST}}. $$ --- Be aware that, the term linear in a OLS model does not mean a linear relationship between the variables, but a model in which the parameters enter the model in a linear way. The following are all linear models (in the parameters): - $y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \ln(x) + \mu$ - $\ln(y) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x + \mu$ - $y = \beta_0 + \beta_1(\frac{1}{x}) + \mu$ --- Implication of the Simple Linear Regression 1. OLS is unbiased _(the proof depends on the four assumptions; if any fail, OLS is not necessarily unbiased.)_ 2. The sampling distribution of our estimates is centered around the true parameter. But, following the second implication, how likely is it that the true slope is slightly larger, smaller or zero? This question can be translated into another assumption: - SLR.5 Homoskedasticity : assume $\text{Var}[\mu | x] = \sigma^2$ Using then the _Homoskedasticity assumption_, we can derive the variance of $b_1$. $$ \text{Var}[\mu | x] = \mathbb{E}[\mu^2 | x] - \mathbb{E}[\mu | x]^2 = \mathbb{E}[\mu^2 | x] =\\ \mathbb{E}[\mu^2] = \text{Var}[\mu] = \sigma^2 $$ Hence, $\sigma^2$ is also the unconditional variance , called the error variance . We can then use this formula to find the variance of $b_1$: $$ \text{Var}(b_1) = \frac{\sigma^2}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i - \bar{x})^2} = \frac{\sigma^2}{SST_x} $$ - the larger the error variance $\sigma^2$, the larger the variance of the slope estimate. - the larger the variability in the $x$ ($SST_x$), the smaller the variance of the slope estimate. Finally, starting from the residuals $\hat u_i$, we can form an unbiased estimate of the error variance (often called the mean squared error (MSE)), denoted by $s^2$: $$ s^2 = (n-2)^{-1} \sum_i \hat u_i^2 = \frac{SSR}{n-2} $$ _Intuition_: $\sigma^2$ is the truth; $s^2$ is our best guess based on the sample we have. Moreover, we divide by $(n-2)$ because, estimating $b_0, b_1$, we lost 2 degrees of freedom. --- 3 Multiple Regression Analysis: Basics The key problem with simple linear regression is that the assumption $\mathbb{E}[u | x] = \mathbb{E}[u] = 0$ is _often problematic_. Consider, for example, that the _true population model_ is: $$ \begin{align*} \text{wage} &= \beta_0 + \beta_1 \text{education} + \beta_2 \text{IQ} + \mu \\ \text{wage} &=\beta_0 + \beta_1 \text{education} + v \end{align*} $$ The previous assumption states that the error term $v = \beta_2 \text{IQ} + \mu$ has $0$ (zero) expected value _(no trend)_ or, in other words, $\text{IQ}$ does not correlate with the $\text{wage}$. This causes $\beta_1$ to be biased compared to the "true parameter", so it _doesn't measure_ the causal effect of education on wage. Thus, instead of assuming that multiple variables are _uncorrelated_ with the output variable, the multiple regression model allows to include them directly in the model. $$ y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \beta_2 x_2 \ldots \beta_k x_k + \mu $$ - $\beta_0$ is still the intercept - $\beta_0 \ldots \beta_k$ are the slope parameters - $\mu$ is the error term. We still need a zero conditional mean : $\mathbb{E}[u | x_1 \ldots x_k] = 0$, that means, in other words, that all the factors that influence the outcome are included in the model. --- In order to estimate the parameters, we still use the Ordinary Least Squares method and we minimize the residuals: $$ \min_{\beta_0 \ldots \beta_k} \sum_i (y_i - \beta_0 - \beta_1 x_1 \ldots \beta_k x_k -\mu)^2 $$ leading to $k + 1$ conditions to derive $k + 1$ parameters. The estimate model $\hat y = b_0 + b_1 x_1 + b_2 x_2 \ldots b_k x_k + \mu$ allows a ceteris paribus interpretation, that means that a change in $x_1$, $\Delta x_1$, leads to a change in $y$ given by $b_1 \Delta x_1$, keeping all the other $x_i$ fixed. --- Frisch-Waugh-Lovell (FWL) Theorem Given a multiple regression model (two regressors, for example) $y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \beta_2 x_2 + \mu$, the coefficient $\beta_1$ can be found in two steps: 1. Regress $x_1$ on $x_2$: $$ x_1 = \alpha_0 + \alpha_1 x_2 + u $$ Save then the residuals $\hat r_1 = x_1 - \hat x_1$. This represents the part of $x_1$ _not correlated_ with $x_2$. 2. Regress $y$ on the residuals: $$ y = \gamma \hat{r}_1 + e $$ The resulting coefficient $\gamma$ will be identical to $b_1$ of the original model. --- The previous definition of $R^2$ is still valid , but we can add the following remarks: - $R^2$ is the squared correlation coefficient between $y$ and the predicted $\hat y$. - $R^2$ never decreases when adding independent variables to a regression (it usually increases). - Because it increases when the number of parameters changes, it is NOT a good measure for comparing different models. As in the simple regression model, we can formalize the assumptions for the multiple one: - MLR.1: Linear in parameters in the population model the following relation holds: $$ y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \cdots + \beta_k x_k + \mu $$ - MLR.2: Random sampling we have a random sample of size $n$: $$ \{(y_i, x_{i1}, \ldots, x_{ik})\}_{i=1}^n $$ - MLR.3: No perfect collinearity (sample variation in explanatory variables) no explanatory variable is an exact linear combination of the others, and each regressor has variation in the sample. - MLR.4: Zero Conditional Mean $$ \mathbb{E}[\mu \mid x_1,\ldots,x_k] = 0 $$ or, for every slice of population determined by $(x_1,\ldots,x_k)$, the average of $\mu$ is zero. This also implies: $$ \mathbb{E}[y \mid x_1,\ldots,x_k] = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \cdots + \beta_k x_k $$ also called the Population Regression Function . Then, we can derive the following: Implication 1: Unbiasedness of OLS Under the previous assumptions, the OLS estimator is unbiased : $\mathbb{E}[b_i] = \beta_i$. - If we include _irrelevant variables_ in our model, the OLS estimator remains still unbiased . - If we exclude _relevant variables_, OLS will usually be biased . Let's suppose the true model is: $$ y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \beta_2 x_2 + \mu $$ but our model is: $$ y = \gamma_0 + \gamma_1 x_1 + v $$ and we actually estimate: $$ y = c_0 + c_1 x_1. $$ The slope parameter will then be: $$ c_1 = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i-\bar{x})(y_i-\bar{y})}{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i-\bar{x})^2} $$ Recall that the numerator is: $$ \begin{align*} & \sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_{i1}-\bar{x}_1)(y_i-\bar{y}) =\\ = &\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_{i1}-\bar{x}_1)\left( \beta_0+\beta_1 x_{i1}+\beta_2 x_{i2}+u_i -\beta_0-\beta_1\bar{x}_1-\beta_2\bar{x}_2 \right) \\ =& \beta_1\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_{i1}-\bar{x}_1)^2 +\beta_2\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_{i1}-\bar{x}_1)(x_{i2}-\bar{x}_2) +\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_{i1}-\bar{x}_1)u_i \end{align*} $$ Since $\mathbb{E}[\mu_i] = 0$, it implies that: $$ \mathbb{E}[c_1] = \beta_1 + \beta_2 \frac{ \sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_{i1}-\bar{x}_1)(x_{i2}-\bar{x}_2) }{ \sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_{i1}-\bar{x}_1)^2 } $$ Note that the term after $\beta_2$ is the slope from the regression of $x_2$ on $x_1$: $$ d_1 = \frac{ \sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_{i1}-\bar{x}_1)(x_{i2}-\bar{x}_2) }{ \sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_{i1}-\bar{x}_1)^2 } $$ so we have: $$ \mathbb{E}[c_1] = \beta_1 + \beta_2 d_1 $$ 0, $d_1$ > 0", "Corr($x_1, x_2$) 0$", "Positive bias", "Negative bias"], ["$\\beta_2 Then we can consider two corner cases: - $\beta_2 = 0$: $x_2$ doesn't affect $y$: - $d_1 = 0$, so $x_1, x_2$ uncorrelated in the sample. --- Assumption 2: Efficiency of OLS Estimator Once we know that the estimate is centered around the true parameter, we want to understand how it is distributed. If we add a fifth assumption ( MLR.5 Homoskedasticity ), we know that: $$ \text{Var}[\mu | x_1, \ldots, x_k] = \mathbb{E}[\mu^2 | x_1, \ldots, x_k] - \mathbb{E}[\mu | x_1, \ldots, x_k]^2 = \sigma^2 $$ we also know/derive that: $$ \text{Var}[y | x_1 \ldots x_k] = \mathbb{E}[y^2 | x_1 \ldots x_k] - \mathbb{E}[y | x_1 \ldots x_k]^2 = \sigma^2 $$ --- Theorem: Sampling Variances of the OLS Slope Estimators Given the assumptions from MLR.1 to MLR.5, $$ \text{Var}[\,\hat b_j\,] \,=\, \frac{\sigma^2}{SST_j\,(1 - R_j^2)} $$ where - $SST_j = \sum_{i=1}^n (x_{i,j} - \bar x_j)^2$ is the total sum of squares of the predictor $x_j$ to its mean $\bar x_j$ ($i$ indexes observations, $j$ indexes the variable) - $R_j^2$ is the $R^2$ from the auxiliary regression of $x_j$ on the other regressors (including the intercept). Intuition and consequences: - a larger $\sigma^2$ increases the variance of the estimator. - a smaller $SST_j$ (less spread in $x_j$) increases the variance of the estimator. - a larger $R_j^2$ (strong linear dependence between $x_j$ and the other regressors) increases the variance of the estimator $\to$ this is the multicollinearity effect. Notes: - If you only have one regressor, drop the $j$ subscript: $SST=\sum_i(x_i-\bar x)^2$ and $R_j^2=0$, recovering $\text{Var}[\hat b]=\sigma^2/SST$. - The Variance Inflation Factor is $\mathrm{VIF}_j = 1/(1-R_j^2) \to$ it quantifies the multiplicative inflation of the variance due to collinearity. --- Let's analyze now what happens if we mis-specify the model. Consider again the true and the mis-specified models: $$ y = c_0 + c_1 x_1 \\ \bar y = b_0 + b_1 x_1 + b_2 x_2 $$ In this case the estimated variance equals $\text{Var}[c_1] = \sigma^2 / SST_1$. On the other hand, the variance using the _true model_ equals: $\text{Var}[b_1] = \frac{ \sigma^2 }{SST_1 ( 1 - R_1^2)}$ So, unless $x_1, x_2$ are uncorrelated: $$ \text{Var}[c_1] --- Estimating the error - Estimate of the error variance $$ s^2 = \hat{\sigma}^2 = \frac{SSR}{df} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}\hat u_i^2}{n-(k+1)}, \qquad df = n-(k+1) $$ where $df$ represents the degrees of freedom. - Standard deviation of $b_j$: $$ \operatorname{sd}(b_j) = \sqrt{\operatorname{Var}(b_j\mid X)} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{SST_j(1-R_j^2)}} $$ - Standard error of $b_j$: $$ \operatorname{se}(b_j) = \frac{s}{\sqrt{SST_j(1-R_j^2)}} $$ --- Theorem: Unbiased Estimation of $\sigma^2$: : Under the assumptions MLR.1 to MLR.5, the $s^2$ estimator is unbiased, $\mathbb{E}[s^2] = \sigma^2$. --- Theorem: Gauss-Markov Theorem Under the assumptions MLR.1 to MLR.5, the OLS estimators $b_0 \ldots b_k$ are the best linear unbiased estimators (BLUEs) of $\beta_0 \ldots \beta_k$: - Best : estimators have the lowest possible variance - Linear : estimators are a linear function of $y$ - Unbiased : expected value equals the population parameters. --- 4 Multiple Regression Analysis: Inference So far, we've seen that, given the MLR 1-5, the OLS is BLUE (most precise, most accurate). To do hypothesis testing, we add another assumption. MLR. 6: Normality (Classical Linear Model [CLM] Assumption): the disturbance $\mu$ is independent of $x_1, ... x_k$ and it is normally distributed with zero mean and variance $\sigma^2$: $\mu \sim N(0, \sigma^2)$. Under this assumption, conditional on the sample values of the independent variables we obtain: $$ b_i \sim N(\beta_i, \text{Var}(b_i)) $$ _(the coefficient estimate is normally distributed around the true beta)_ This implies that: $$ \frac{b_i - \beta_j }{ \text{std. dev.}(b_j) } \sim N(0,1) $$ _(the standardized average deviation from the true value is standardized normal)_ Furthermore, if we use the estimate $s^2$ of the variance of the disturbance ($\sigma^2$), under the CLM assumptions we obtain: $$ \frac{(b_j - \beta_j)}{\text{se}(b_j)} \sim t_{n-k-1} $$ where $\text{se}_{b_j} = s / \sqrt{SST_j (1 - R_j^2)}$ and $n-k-1$ is the degree of freedom. This result will be useful to determine how likely an estimate $b_j$ is similar to $\beta_j$. Both the $t$-Student and the Normal distribution are symmetric bell-shaped but the $t$-Student: - has fatter tails than the normal - converges to the normal for an infinite sample - is conditional to the degree of freedom - can be approximated with a normal distribution where the degree of freedom $> 30$ or similar. !Population Model --- Test Hypotheses Before starting with the test, let's take a look to the different errors: - Type I Error : we reject the null hypothesis when it is true _(false positive)_. $$ \alpha = P(H_0 \text{ rejected when it is true }) $$ - Type II Error : we don't reject the null hypothesis when it is false _(false negative)_. $$ \beta = P(\text{failing to reject } H_0 \text{when it is false}) $$ --- $t$ Test 1. Set up the hypothesis. $H_0, H_1$ can be one-sided ($H_0: b_j \le 0, H_1: b_j > 0$) or two sided ($H_0: b_j = 0, H_1: b_j \neq 0$). 2. Determine the $t$-statistic using the estimates for $b_j, se(b_j)$. _For example, as seen before:_ $t = \frac{b_j - 0}{se(b_j)}$ 3. Select a _significance level_, or, in other terms, the chance to make a Type I error and determine the _critical value_, depending if it is a one- or two-sided hypothesis. 4. Decide: reject $H_0$ if the absolute value of the $t$-statistic is larger than the critical value. For example, let's consider the following model: $$ \text{employment} = b_0 + b_1 \text{education} + ... + b_k x_{ik} + \mu_i $$ 1. We set up the hypothesis: $H_0: b_1 \le 0, H_1: b_1 > 0$ _($H_0$: education doesn't increase employment)_ 2. We calculate $t = b_1 / se(b_1)$ 3. We consider $c$ using $\alpha$ (confidence level) and degree of freedom $= n - k - 1$ - $c$ is the ($1 - \alpha / 2)$ quantile of the distribution of the test for a two-sided test - $c$ is the $(1-\alpha)$ quantile for an upper-tail test - $c$ is the $\alpha$ quantile for a lower-tail test 4. We reject $H_0$ if $t > c$ _(if $H_0: b_1 > 0$ use $t !Population Model !Population Model Summary: - A coefficient is significant at the $\alpha$% level when its estimate is _large relative to its standard error_ $\implies$ when its absolute $t$-statistic is large enough. - Do not confuse the size of an estimate with significance: _a large effect can be imprecise, and a small effect can still be precisely estimated._ - Significance answers a hypothesis-testing question _(it does not tell you whether the effect is economically important)_ - When a question asks for the number of significant coefficients, count the coefficients that reject the null individually, _not the joint significance of the whole regression._ - The intercept is tested exactly like any other coefficient $\implies$ include it unless the question explicitly excludes it. - If a coefficient is not significant $\implies$ _data do not provide enough evidence against the null at the chosen level._ More generally, we can test whether an estimate fits a specific value: $H_0: b_j = a$. In this case, we use the appropriate $t$-statistic: $t = (b_j - a)/se(b_j)$, where $a = 0$ for the standard test. --- Confidence Intervals Another way to use statistical testing is to construct confidence intervals using the same critical value for a two-sided test. A $(1 - \alpha)\%$ confidence interval is defined as $b_j \pm c \cdot se(b_j)$ where $c$ is the $(1 - \alpha / 2)$ percentile in a $t_{n-k-1}$ distribution. P-Values for $t$-Tests An alternative approach is to calculate what is the _smallest significance level_ at which the null hypothesis would be rejected given the data. So we compute the $t$-statistic and we look up at which percentile it is in the appropriate $t$-distribution. This is called the p-value . The p-value represents the probability that we would observe the $t$-statistic we did, if the null were true. Testing more complex hypotheses - Linear Combination Suppose we want to test if $b_1$ is equal to another parameter, that is: $H_0: b_1 = b_2$. Then the statistic test is $ t = (b_1 - b_2)/se(b_1 - b_2)$. But, if we expand the formula: $$ se(b_1 - b_2) = \sqrt{\text{Var}(b_1 - b_2)} =\sqrt{\text{Var}(b_1)+\text{Var}(b_2)-2\text{CoV}(b_1,b_2)} =\\ =\sqrt{se(b_1)^2 + se(b_2)^2-2s_{12}} $$ So we need $s_{12}$, which we don't usually have. To avoid this, we can use the following _"trick"_. We set $H_0: q_1 = b_1 - b_2 = 0$. To do that, we also have to substitute $q_1 = b_1 - b_2$ in our model. So, for example, we can consider: $$ y = b_0 + b_1 x_1 + b_2 x_2 + \mu \implies \\ y = b_0 + q_1 x_1 + b_2 (x_1 + x_2) + \mu $$ Multiple Linear Restrictions So far we tested a single linear restriction ($b_1 = 0, b_1 = b_2$). Now we want to jointly test multiple hypotheses about the parameters. A typical example is testing _"exclusion restrictions"_, that means knowing if a group of parameters are equal to zero. Then the null hypothesis might be something like: $H_0: b_{k-q+1} = 0 = ... = b_k = 0$, but we cannot check each statistic separately because we want to know if the $q$ parameters are jointly significant. We instead need to estimate: - the restricted model without all the $x_{k-q+1}..x_k$ - the unrestricted model with all the $x$ included. _Intuition: we want to know if the change in SSR is big enough_. This is called the $F$-statistic, defined as: $$ F = \frac{(SSR_r - SSR_{ur})/q}{SSR_{ur}/(n-k-1)} $$ where $r$ is the restricted , $ur$ the unrestricted . _Note that the $F$ statistic is always positive_. _Intuition: the $F$ statistic is measuring the relative increase in the SSR when moving from the unrestricted to the restricted model._ $q = df_r - dr_{ur}, \quad df_{ur} = n - k - 1$. To decide if the increases in SSR is _"big enough"_, to reject the exclusions, we compare if with the $F$ distribution, indeed we know that: $F \sim F_{q, n-k-1}$ where: - $q$ is the numerator degrees of freedom - $n-k-q$ is the denominator degrees od freedom. !f testl --- OLS asymptotics Under the Gauss-Markov assumptions the OLS is BLUE; however they are not always fulfilled with real data. Large samples _(big data!)_ come to our rescue! It can be shown that some nice properties remain intact if $n \to \infty$. _(Larger samples allow us to relax some assumptions)_. - Consistency : if $n \to \infty$ estimators are consistent, that means that the distribution of the estimator collapses to the parameter value. This implies that when $n \to \infty$ we can use MLR.4 (zero mean and zero correlation). Just as we derived the omitted variable bias earlier, we can think about the inconsistency _(asymptotic bias)_. Consider the true model $y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \beta_2 x_2 + v$ and the estimated model: $y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \mu$, so that $\mu = \beta_2 x_2 + v$. In this case, $b_1 = \beta_1 + \beta_2 \delta$, where $\delta = \frac{\operatorname{Cov}(x_1, x_2)}{\operatorname{Var}(x_1)}$, which tells us how much our estimate ($b_1$) deviates from the true parameter ($\beta_1$). _Intuition: inconsistecy is a large sample problem: it does not go away as we add data_. --- Large Sample Inference So far, we relied on assumption about normal distribution of errors, but this assumption can often break down! Again, large samples come to the rescue: as $n \to \infty$, the central limit theorem shows that OLS estimates are asymptotically normal. Thus, we no longer need to assume normality with a large sample, we get it anyway. If $\mu$ is not normally distributed, we sometimes will refer to the standard error as the asymptotic standard error . In general, we can expect standard errors to shrink at a rate proportional to the inverse of $\sqrt{n}$. Asymptotic Efficiency There are other estimators besides OLS that are consistent. However, under the Gauss-Markov assumptions, the OLS estimators will have the smallest asymptotic variances; therefore we say that OLS is _asymptotically_ efficient. --- 5 Multiple Regression Analysis: Further Issues To test hypotheses about estimates, we previously relied on assumption about normal distribution of errors (MLR.6) in order to redive $t$ and $F$ distributions. This implied that the distribution of $y$ given $x$ was normal as well. However, this assumption about normality can often break down! _(example: a clearly skewed variable, like wages, arrests, savings etc., cannot be normal, since normal distributions are symmetric)._ Also in this case, large samples are the solution: if $n \to \infty$ the Central Limit Theorem shows that OLS estimates are _asymptotically_ normal. _In other terms_, for any population with mean $\mu$ and standard deviation $\sigma$, the sampling distribution of the sample mean is _approx_ normal with mean $\mu$ and standard deviation $\sigma / \sqrt{n}$. Secondly, the $t$-distributoin approaches a normal distribution for a large $df$ (degree of freedom), so we no longer need to assume normality with a large sample. As we said, if the error is NOT _normally_ distributed, we sometimes will refer to it as the _asymptotic standard error_. _(We can expect standard errors to shrink at rate proportional to the inverse of $\sqrt{n}$)_. --- Further Issues in Multiple Regression Analysis: Scaling Variables Changing the scale of the $y$ variable will lead to a change in the scale of the coefficients and the standard error, without a meaningful change in significance/interpretation. The same applies for a change in $x$. _Occasionally_, we will see references to standardized coefficient , calculated using the standardized version of $x,y$, so coefficients reflect a standard deviation change of $y,x$. Functional Form OLS can also be used for relationship that are not strictly linear in $x,y$ by using non-linear functions of $x,y$ _(as long the model is linear in the parameters)_. Log Models - they are invariant to the scale of the variables since it's all about percentage changes. - they give a direct estimate of the _elasticity_ - for models with $y > 0$, the conditional distribution is often heteroskedastic or skewed, while $\ln(y)$ is often less so. - the distribution of $\ln(y)$ is narrower, limiting the effect of outliers. _Note that, when using the log form, variables have to be positive!_ Quadratic Models For a model like $y = \beta_1 x_1 + \beta_2 x_2^2 +\mu$ we know that: - if $\beta_1$ is positive and $\beta_2$ is negative, $y$ is increasing in $x$ at first and then decreasing. - if $\beta_1$ is negative and $\beta_2$ is positive the opposite happens. - The turning point is calculated setting the derivative to zero and lies at: $x^* = - \beta_1 / \beta_2$ --- AdjustedR-Squared Recall that $R^2$ always increases as more variables are added to the model since $SSR$ never decreases with more variables: $$ R^2 = \frac{SSE}{SST} = 1 - \frac{SSR}{SST} $$ The adjusted $R^2$ takes then into account the number of variables in the model: $$ \text{Adj.} R^2 = 1 - \frac{SSR}{SST}\frac{(n-1)}{(n-k-1)} $$ The adjusted $R^2$ penalizes models with more variables, especially for low $n$ and high $k$, and increases only when additional variables are added whose $t$-statistic is larger than $\approx 1$. This means adding variables with poor explanatory power decreases adjusted $R^2$. In other terms, we usually use adjusted $R^2$ to compare models with the same $y$, but never with models with different $y$. (Standard Errors for) Predictions Suppose we want to use our estimates to obtain a specific prediction at a point $c=(c_1,\ldots,c_k)$. The conditional mean is $$ \mathbb{E}(y \mid x_1 = c_1, \ldots, x_k = c_k) = b_0 + b_1 c_1 + \cdots + b_k c_k. $$ To assess the precision of this predicted mean, compute the standard error $$ \operatorname{se}\big(\hat y(c)\big)=\sqrt{\operatorname{Var}\big(b_0 + b_1 c_1 + \cdots + b_k c_k\big)}, $$ which can be obtained from the covariance matrix of the OLS estimates. The standard error for a new observation also adds the error variance $\sigma^2$. --- Regression with Dummy Variables A dummy (binary) variable is a variable that takes the value 1 or 0. Consider a simple model with one continuous variable $x$ and a dummy $D$: $$ y = \beta_0 + \delta_0 D + \beta_1 x_1 + \mu. $$ - $D=1$ if female, $0$ otherwise. - $x$: education in years. - $y$: wage. This can be interpreted as an intercept shift: - if $D=0$ then $y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \mu$ (male, base group). - if $D=1$ then $y = (\beta_0 + \delta_0) + \beta_1 x_1 + \mu$ (female). !Population Model --- Dummies for Multiple Categories Any categorial variable can be turned into a set of dummy variables. Because the base group is represented by the intercept, if there are $n$ categories there should be $n-1$ dummy variables. _Note that we can model interaction between dummies to divide in subgroups and between dummies with continuous variables $x$ to model a change in slope._ !Dummy Vars Testing for Differences Across Groups Testing whether a regression function is different for one group versus another can be thought of as _testing for the joint significance of the dummy and its interactions_ with all other $x$ variables. So we can estimate the model _with_ and _without_ the interactions and form a $F$-statistic _(very tedious in practise!)_. The Chow Test We can compute the $F$-statistic without running the unrestricted model with all interactions with $k$ continuous variables, but intead we can: - run the restricted model for group 1 _(using observations $n_1$)_ and get $SSR_1$ - run the restricted model for group 2 _(using observations $n_2$)_ and get $SSR_2$ - run the restricted model for all _(using $N = n_1 + n_2$)_ and get $SSR$ - compute the $F$-statistic as: $$ F = \frac{[SSR - (SSR_1 + SSR_2)]}{SSR_1 + SSR_2} \frac{n-2(k + 1)}{k + 1} $$ knowing that $F \sim F_{k+1, N - 2(k+1)}$. We then set $H_0$ as "all coefficientsd are equal agross groups", that rewrites as: $$ H_0 : \beta_0(1) = \beta_0(2), \beta_1(1) = \beta_1(2) = ... = \beta_k(1) = \beta_k(2) $$ then we compute $F$ as defined above and we set $c$ as the $(1 - \alpha)$ upper quantile of $F_{k+1, N − 2(k+1)}£$. If $F > c$, we reject $H_0$ at the $\alpha$ significance level _(note that $F$ is an upper-tail test since it's non-negative)_. Dummy as Dependent Value: Linear Probability Model $\mathbb{P}(y = 1|x) = \mathbb{E}[y | x]$ when $y$ is binary model, so we can write our model as: $$ \mathbb{ P}(y = 1|x) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + ... + \beta_k x_k + \mu $$ - $\beta_j$ represents the change in the probability when $x_j$ changes by 1 _around the mean_. - the predicted $y$ is the probability _However_, potential problems arise since the prediction can be outside $[0,1]$. Also, this model will violate the assumption of homoskedasticity, which will affect inferece. _Despite everything_, OLS is usually a good starting point when $y$ is binary. --- 6 Heteroskedasticity and Other Problems Assumptions Multiple Linear Regressin (MLR) Model - MLR.1: Linear in parameters - In the population model, the following relationship holds: $$ y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \cdots + \beta_k x_k + u $$ - MLR.2: Random sampling - We have a random sample of size $n$: $$ \{(y_i, x_{i1}, \ldots, x_{ik})\}_{i=1}^{n} $$ - MLR.3: No perfect collinearity - None of the independent variables is constant, and there are no exact linear relationships among regressors. - MLR.4: Zero conditional mean - The error has zero conditional mean: $$ \mathbb{E}[u \mid x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_k] = 0 $$ - This implies $\mathbb{E}[u] = 0$. - MLR.5: Homoskedasticity - Assume constant conditional variance of the error: $$ \operatorname{Var}(u \mid x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_k) = \sigma^2 $$ - MLR.6: Normality - The disturbance is independent of $x_1,\ldots,x_k$ and normally distributed with zero mean and variance $\sigma^2$: $$ u \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2) $$ Recall that the assumption of _homoskedasticity_ implied that, _conditional on the explanatory variables_, the variance of the unobserved error $\mu$ was constant . If this is not true, then the variance is different for different values of $x$ and errors are said to be heteroskedastic . !Population Model OLS is still unbiased and consistent , even if we do not assume homoskedasticity. _However_, the standard errors of the estimates are biased if we have heteroskedasticity. - If the standard errors are biased, we cannot do inferece based on the usual $t$, $F$, $LM$-statistic. _(The $LM$-statisitc is $LM: n R^2_{\mu}$, and $R^2_{\mu}$ is obtained regressing $\mu$ on all variables. The LM statistic has a $X^2_k$-distributio.)_ --- Variance with Heteroskedasticity For the simple bivariate case , heteroskedasticity implies that: OLS slope decomposition: $$ b_1=\beta_1+\frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i-\bar{x})u_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i-\bar{x})^2} $$ Conditional variance of $b_1$ under heteroskedasticity: $$ \operatorname{Var}(b_1\mid X)= \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i-\bar{x})^2\sigma_i^2}{(SST_x)^2}, \qquad SST_x=\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i-\bar{x})^2 $$ _Note that this differs from the homoskedastic case_ $$ \operatorname{Var}(b_1\mid X)=\frac{\sigma^2}{SST_x} $$ A valid _(consistent)_ estimator of the variance of $b_1$ when $\sigma_i^2\neq\sigma^2$ is: $$ \widehat{\operatorname{Var}}(b_1)= \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i-\bar{x})^2\hat{u}_i^2}{(SST_x)^2} $$ where $\hat{u}_i$ are the OLS residuals. _Note that this is different from the homoskedastic estimator_: $$ \widehat{\operatorname{Var}}(b_1)= \frac{\frac{1}{n-2}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\hat{u}_i^2}{SST_x} = \frac{SSR}{(n-2)SST_x} $$ --- Robust Standard Errors Consider the model: $$ y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \cdots + \beta_k x_k + u $$ With heteroskedasticity, a valid _(consistent)_ estimator of $\operatorname{Var}(b_j)$ is: $$ \widehat{\operatorname{Var}}(b_j) = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} r_{ij}^{\,2}\hat{u}_i^{\,2}}{\left(SSR_j\right)^2} $$ where: - $r_{ij}$ is the $i$-th residual from regressing $x_j$ on all other independent variables. - $SSR_j = \sum_{i=1}^{n} r_{ij}^{\,2}$ is the sum of squared residuals from this auxiliary regression. - $\hat{u}_i$ are the OLS residuals from the original model. So, the corresponding robust standard error is: $$ \operatorname{se}_{rob}(b_j)=\sqrt{\widehat{\operatorname{Var}}(b_j)} $$ Sometimes a finite-sample correction is used: $$ \widehat{\operatorname{Var}}_{corr}(b_j) = \frac{n}{n-k-1}\,\widehat{\operatorname{Var}}(b_j) $$ As $n \to \infty$, this correction becomes negligible. _Note that robust standard errors are justified asymptotically. In small samples, $t$-statistics based on robust SE may not be close to a $t$ distribution._ --- Testing for Heteroskedasticity We want to test: $$ H_0:\;\operatorname{Var}(\mu\mid x_1,\ldots,x_k)=\sigma^2\quad\text{for all }(x_1,\ldots,x_k). $$ This statement is equivalent to $$ \mathbb{E}[\mu^2\mid x_1,\ldots,x_k]=\sigma^2 $$ only under the additional (usual) assumption that $$ \mathbb{E}[\mu\mid x_1,\ldots,x_k]=0, $$ since $$ \operatorname{Var}(\mu\mid x)=\mathbb{E}[\mu^2\mid x]-\big(\mathbb{E}[\mu\mid x]\big)^2. $$ In practice we typically assume $\mathbb{E}[\mu\mid x]=0$, so testing whether $\mathbb{E}[\mu^2\mid x]$ is constant across $x$ is a valid way to test homoskedasticity. If we assume the relationship betwee $\mu^2$ and $x_j$ to be linear, we can test it as a _linea restriction_: $$ \mu^2 = \delta_0 + \delta_1 x_1 + ... + \delta_k x_k + v \iff H_0: \delta_1 = ... = \delta_k = 0 $$ The Breush-Pagan Test In this test, we do not observe the error, but we can estimate it with the residuals from the OLS regrssion. _After regressing the residuals squared on all $x$_, we can use the $R^2$ to form a $F$ or $LM$-test. - The $F$-statistic is distributed as $F_{k,n-k-1}$ and it is equal to: $$ F = \frac{R^2 (n-k-1)}{(1-R^2)k} $$ - THe $LM$-statistic follows a $X^2_k$-distribution and is: $$ LM = nR^2 $$ _Note that the Bresuch-Pagan test detect any linear forms of heteroskedasticity ._ --- The White Test The White Test allows for general _(including non-linear)_ forms of heteroskedasticity by using squares and cross‑products of the regressors _(or by using functions of the fitted values)._ We can proceed in two ways: -
