To delve deeper into the next topic, however, we have to bring in surgeons and extreme sports. !Risk Plot Having food in the pantry as a simple application of the Redundancy Principle Bungee Jumping, Surgeons, and Skin in The Game Bungee jumping has historically caused $1$ death for every $500,000$ jumps. A very low rate. The problem is that for that poor desperate person whose rope breaks, statistics don't matter: 100% of the bad luck falls on them irreversibly. The same goes for surgery. We believe in modern medicine because, _on average_, it saves lives. But when the surgeon messes up an operation, the patient doesn't console themselves by thinking about how many healed smiles leave the hospital every day. These two examples, deliberately extreme, serve to clarify an important lesson: in the presence of non-ergodic phenomena (or, in other words, in the face of irreversible risks), the very concept of classical probability and arithmetic mean lose their meaning for the individual. When an individual faces an irreversible and unmanageable loss due to an event $X$, their individual utility (happiness) is irremediably compromised. This is true regardless of the fact that, _on average_, event $X$ increases the utility (happiness) of the population. Since it is a loss with no chance of recovery and no insurance, the average benefit enjoyed by others becomes irrelevant to them. Generalizing, one could also conclude that the survival of a system rarely coincides with the survival of its individuals and that, often, the system has no incentive for the survival of its members. On the contrary, the replaceability of individuals makes the system ergodic, antifragile, or capable of evolving _(depending on the author you read: Dell’Anna, Taleb, or Darwin)._ The situation changes radically if the loss is manageable . In that case, risk-sharing or transfer mechanisms _(p2p, insurance policies)_ can protect the individual, allowing them to still benefit from a potential increase in their utility. The surgeon's example, moreover, highlights the problem of "skin in the game" _(as Taleb defines it)_. In many situations, the entity making the decision _(example: a politician)_ does not coincide with the entity upon which the irreversible negative consequences fall _(example: the citizens of a country)_. For the hospital system, a death caused by avoidable errors is one number among millions of patients _(ergodic situation)_. For the unfortunate patient, on the contrary, it is a 100% ruin event _(non-ergodic phenomenon)_. The solution to this misalignment problem suggests acting in contexts where those who make decisions _(friends, politicians, companies, etc. etc.)_ coincide with those who pay the consequences of bad choices but, due to obvious real-world dynamics, such behavior is almost never possible. !Risk Plot Politicians (and voters) often don't have skin in the game, but pizza makers do: offering a bad pizza to a customer negatively affects their profits. Always look up when walking down the street This entire discussion on risk, if taken to the extreme, would also lead one to never leave the house for fear of a ceramic pot falling on their head. The truth is that our brain constantly performs a cost-benefit analysis, evaluating, more or less rationally, the probabilities, benefits, and utility of every situation we expose ourselves to. As a general and purely theoretical rule, our risk aversion should be: - Infinite for risks in Quadrant 3 _(irreversible and uninsurable)_. - High for Quadrant 4 _(irreversible but insurable)_, with a concave utility function - Low for Quadrant 1 and 2 _(reversible risks)_, with linear utility functions _(if we are purely interested in the expected value)_ or convex _(if there is an asymmetry in our favor, see Taleb's barbell strategy for more on this )_. !Risk Plot Me trying to imitate Taleb's barbell strategy. Find the right friends The last risks I would like to address are the most subjective, difficult to insure, and often irreversible: those related to human capital and relationships. Regarding the former, the greatest risk is not a realized loss, but a potential loss , namely the failure to exploit growth opportunities. The most trivial example comes from my university experience. In a grading system from 0 to 30 _(with 18 being a pass, I know: the italian grading system is strange...)_, it is much more useful to get two 23s on two exams than a 30 and a 17, even though the arithmetic average is lower in the first scenario. - Case $A$: $23 + 23$ $\to$ Average $23$ _(the university path continues)_ - Case $B$: $30 + 17$ $\to$ Average $23.5$ _(the failure introduces a high cost of failure: extra time, effort, and stress)_ The discussion becomes more complex with relationships , as emotions and interpersonal dynamics come into play. I will just say that, of all risks, ending an important relationship is perhaps the most impactful, irreversible, and least insurable event of our lives. In this case, however, not feeling up to the task of an in-depth discussion as before, I will limit myself to proposing, _rather hastily_, two possible solutions: - apply the same logic as before: prefer "ruin minimization" (avoiding irreparable breakups, lies, betrayals...) to "average maximization" (being right most of the time, getting along at all costs...). - have friends who are not easily offended Mine, _fortunately_, not only fall into this category, but _(more importantly)_ they put up with me even when I'm in a bad mood because of my tendon pain. Thanks! !Amici Zurigo And if, besides putting up with me, they come to Zurich to visit me, they are definitely the right ones! Bibliography - Ergodicity, Luca Dellanna: the book - Ergodicity Economics - Skin in the game by Taleb - Short video explanation: ergodicity - Very short video explanation: ergodicity - The LoR Podcast: What is risk? (ITA) - The LoR Podcast: Ergodicity and Risk (ITA) - Too big to fail Podcast: Interview with Luca Dellanna (ITA) - Luca Dellanna & Mr.Rip (very old but gold) (ITA) --- Appendix A.1 - Why does my cousin expect to win only 0.71 races? Let's define the variables for a single race $i$: - $Y_i$: My cousin finishes race $i$ without falling - $X_i$: My cousin wins race $i$ - $S_i$: My cousin has survived (not broken his leg) until the start of race $i$ From the text, we get: $$ P(Y_i = 1) = 1 - 0.2 = 0.8 $$ $$ P(X_i=1 \mid Y_i=1) = 0.2 $$ since the probability of winning is conditional on having reached the finish line (event $Y_i=1$). Therefore, the absolute probability of winning race $i$, assuming he started (event $S_i$), is: $$ P(\text{Win Race } i \mid S_i) = P(X_i=1 \mid Y_i=1) \cdot P(Y_i=1) $$ $$ P(\text{Win Race } i \mid S_i) = 0.2 \cdot 0.8 = \mathbf{0.16} $$ Every time he is at the starting gate, my cousin therefore has a 16% chance of winning and an 80% chance of being able to participate in the next race. Furthermore, the probability of getting to participate in race $i$ (event $S_i$) depends on not having fallen in any of the $i-1$ previous races. Consequently: $$ P(S_i) = (0.8)^{i-1} $$ We can therefore write that the absolute probability of winning race $i$ _(seen from the start of the championship)_ is the probability of surviving until that race ($P(S_i)$) multiplied by the probability of winning it once there (0.16). $$ P(X_i = 1) = P(\text{Win Race } i \mid S_i) \cdot P(S_i) $$ $$ P(X_i = 1) = 0.16 \cdot (0.8)^{i-1} $$ The total number of expected wins, $\mathbb{E}[V]$, is the sum of the probabilities of winning each single race, from $i=1$ to $i=10$. $$ \mathbb{E}[V] = \sum_{i=1}^{10} P(X_i = 1) = \sum_{i=1}^{10} 0.16 \cdot (0.8)^{i-1} $$ which is nothing more than a geometric series with first term $a = 0.16$ and ratio $r = 0.8$. $$ \mathbb{E}[V] = a \cdot \left( \frac{1 - r^n}{1 - r} \right) = 0.16 \cdot \left( \frac{1 - 0.8^{10}}{1 - 0.8} \right) $$ $$ \mathbb{E}[V] = 0.16 \cdot \left( \frac{1 - 0.8^{10}}{0.2} \right) $$ $$ \mathbb{E}[V] = 0.8 \cdot (1 - 0.8^{10}) $$ $$ \mathbb{E}[V] \approx 0.8 \cdot (1 - 0.1074) \approx 0.8 \cdot 0.8926 $$ $$\mathbb{E}[V] \approx \mathbf{0.714} $$ A.2 - Alternative Definitions of Ergodicity? An observable event is called ergodic if its expectation value is constant in time and its time average converges to this value with probability one. _[Ole Peters, Alexander Adamou]_ Ergodicity holds if, more or less, every time you compute a statistical measure (in space or in time), you find the same result. It means that the randomness is "well-distributed". Another way to see it is that, when there is ergodicity, doing $N$ random experiments in parallel gives the same result as doing $N$ experiments in series.

notes.title: Why Switzerland?
notes.fullText: Watch Out for the Tram! I remember my first week in Zurich very well. At dinner with a friend, also a student who had moved to the same city more than a year prior, I confessed: _"I'm still in the phase where everything seems wow to me"_. He replied quite naturally and ironically: _"Don't worry, for me this phase has lasted a year and shows no signs of ending"_. Almost six months later, despite knowing Switzerland better and being almost used to a new life, I can't help but agree with him. I often repeat that I want to die in Zurich _(preferably not hit by a tram within a few months)_ but, while keeping numerous alternatives open, I admit that, at this time, Zurich represents an ideal dimension. I was (and remain) convinced that living and working in Switzerland represents a true arbitrage compared to the rest of Europe , and I cannot understand why this “disparity” _(or market inefficiency)_ is not exploited _(and consequently hedged)_ by the rest of the continent. The reality is that (fortunately) most of the population does not think like me: for many, Switzerland is not a model to imitate or a destination to aspire to; rather, it is a country that is too difficult , far from European ideals , and driven by a society very different from the Italian one. !I have a bed! _September 2025: my father and I setting up the new room - February 2026: after 6 months I decide to buy and assemble the bed_ What does it feel like to be a millionaire in Switzerland? Nothing, there is no shame in being poor When I was little, the stereotype of the average Swiss person was that of a middle-aged individual, socially reserved, rigid about quiet hours, and likely the holder of an _(at least)_ 7-figure bank account. The truth is that, while these people do exist, Zurich, especially among the younger generations, is fortunately a much more heterogeneous and multicultural city than I expected. Behind the caricature just mentioned, however, lies what, at least in my experience, represents the founding value of this country: respect. The world is a beautiful place because it is diverse In short, it is certainly not my intention to create a ranking of European countries or to prove the superiority of Switzerland; rather, I want to tell the story of how people with different priorities look for different contexts , forming different societies that attract like-minded personalities and fuel a feedback loop capable of strengthening the cultural identity of each country. To conclude, take some time to play with the Better Life Index [OECD] , an intuitive online tool that allows you to weight the aspects of life you consider most important and identify the countries best aligned with your preferences! Cheers!

notes.title: Should society tell you that you are useless?
notes.fullText: _An article exceptionally co-written: Giulia, whom you will meet shortly, and I have had strong fights discussions on this topic._ _The paragraphs were written separately to preserve two distinct visions. It is up to the reader to figure out who wrote what._ _PS: If you like Giulia, check out her Substack. _ Polimi, ETH & Statale I was lucky enough to grow up with the awareness that my passions coincided, _at least for the most part_, with skills that the job market rewarded and that, more broadly, society considered useful. Since I was a child, I always showed an interest in mathematics, physics, and technology, discovering the world of coding on my own and ending up, _inevitably_, studying Engineering at the Politecnico di Milano. Later, becoming passionate about finance and economics in my spare time, I moved to the dark side by starting a master's in Management and Economics at ETH. Only now, as I get closer to leaving the happy status of a student and after numerous discussions with different perspectives and hopes, do many doubts arise. More specifically, my reflections come from long debates held with two wise women who, _fortunately_, have a great influence on my life _(my mother and my girlfriend, Giulia)_ and who, ironically, also share, with some time delay, the same course of study: both studied philosophy. The central theme of our conversations, and consequently of these lines, can be summarized as: “How and in what way should society tell you how useless you are?” !Skills Matching! Comparing desks: the engineer and the classicist. Separated at Birth During my _(almost)_ 20 years of study, there has always been a constant: I knew that what I was studying coincided with something “useful” and, consequently, following society’s most cliché and overused advice, I “followed my passions.” Now imagine a twin brother of mine who, starting from the same situation and equal boundary conditions _(same intelligence, stupidity, and access to opportunities)_, shows interest in different aspects of knowledge _(read: philosophy, epistemology, ethics)_ and, following the same advice I did, ends up studying humanities. It can reasonably be expected that, regardless of the graduation grade or the effort put into studying during the academic path, one of the two will have an easier entry into the world of work, a better-paid position, and a higher level of professional satisfaction. I am also convinced that, since there is much more demand for STEM figures on the market and consequently a higher probability for them to be hired in a similar sector, holding a job position more or less aligned with what they studied (which consequently “values” a student’s academic path) leads to greater levels of satisfaction. On the contrary, following a humanities path with great passion but ending up, due to low demand, doing something _NOT_ related to one's studies, can be much more frustrating and demotivating. _(And by this, I don't just mean the classic stereotype of the philosopher preparing fries at * insert American fast-food name * , but all those graduates who, not knowing what career to pursue, end up taking the first job they find, partly as a fallback, partly out of the necessity to make a living)._ !Skills Matching! We remain big fans of french fries, but we hope to have nothing to do with them professionally. Almost in the Relegation Zone Strongly influenced by my background, at this moment in my life, I feel I can answer the same question with a very convinced: “maybe.” Mine is the experience of a twenty-two-year-old who, _excluding a brief stint of wanting to become an astronaut_, has always had interests in the "humanities" field and followed a "humanities" school path, culminating in the choice of Philosophy as a university faculty. The _“do what you love and things will follow”_ or _“follow your passions”_ are therefore narratives that I have been and still am quite familiar with, and which have seen me direct a wide spectrum of feelings toward them: from trust that it was true; to disillusionment that it was an excessive romanticism that, however, deviates from reality; to “maybe.” Now, in this very delicate phase where “do what you like” must inevitably take on an increasingly concrete form, I find myself thinking that, _however appropriately warned by the stereotypes about studying Philosophy_, this narrative should have been accompanied by a good dose of pragmatism. Due to the strong skill mismatch between supply and demand , a large portion of non-STEM graduates ends up, out of necessity, occupying generic positions not designed for their course of study, where the latter is not valued in the slightest. On the contrary, only the brightest _(and luckiest!)_ percentile of humanities graduates ends up having an intentional career path that is especially connected to their background, without having to adapt and accept compromises. !Skills Matching! Skill matching: comparison of EU countries, 2024. Source: CEDEFOP, EU . Philosophy has this use: it serves to console us for its uselessness. The evolution of the simple question _“and after graduation?”_ has become: _“in today's world, do spaces exist for figures with an education not oriented toward a practice?”_ In a way of conceiving education as increasingly necessary and hyper-specialized , is there a need for figures who, in fact, don't know how to do anything useful? How much room should be left for reinterpretation? And how much does this reinventing oneself lead to compromises where one's profile and experience are not valued? Starting from these realities and the central question posed by Giulia, I have broken down the problem into two main points, much more related than they might seem: - In what way and at what stage of growth should society _(and who, within society)_ give us realistic perspectives on what awaits us after our studies? - Is following your passions always a good idea? Bad Advice Reflecting in hindsight on my academic path, only a few times(_probably too late and never in a clear way)_, was I given a realistic picture of the skills that the job market rewards _(or is expected to reward)_. The little awareness I have gained over the years comes, almost exclusively, from the wise advice _(and the right impositions)_ given by my parents, who always “gently pushed” me in a more or less successful direction. The primary cause of all this is that the teachers we grow up with _(who are also the people best suited to shape our mindset)_ are, by necessity, incapable and therefore ineffective (almost never through their own fault) at doing all this. Firstly, the (Italian) school system does not provide a path aimed at providing awareness of the post-academic world and, _only more strictly_, professional integration. Secondly, and probably much more importantly, teachers, _while being much more cultured and prepared in their field than I could ever hope to be in my life,_ have likely spent most of their careers within the walls of a school, remaining not very permeable to how the job market has evolved, especially in recent years. Although all the possible answers to my questions are not binary but rather fall on a spectrum between romanticism and brutal honesty , I remain convinced that, at least in Italy, we remain too idealistic about it. I still think it's part of everyone's growth process to discover how to be useful to society _(or, more concretely, in which sector to professionalize and look for work)_; however, most students (except for the lucky ones who receive input in other contexts) lack the tools and sufficient initiative to do so. This is why schools, especially through figures outside the purely academic world, should provide better guidance through these uncertainties. !Skills Matching! Our respective very first days of school... when the working world seemed unreachable! My answer to the second question is strongly influenced by a book I read a few years ago but whose thesis, fortunately, often resonates within me: So Good They Can’t Ignore You by Cal Newport. The book's main thesis is that following your passions, _besides being misleading_, leads nowhere. On the contrary, competence and a sense of impact are what positively correlate with a satisfying future (and career). The advice _“follow your passions"_ thus translates to _“find what you are good at, specialize, and look for a job in that vertical.”_ In fact, I am convinced that being recognized and valued, from both a human and professional perspective, for one's skill, competence, or knowledge (even if it doesn't correspond to our first and greatest passion) is much more satisfying than seeing one's effort poorly appreciated by society, _regardless of how passionate one might be about it._ !Skills Matching! Snapshots of writing and reading together daily Italy is a country of the old Trying instead to look for a historical reason why the idea of _“do what you love”_ is so widespread (and starting from the premise that this statement is addressed to a segment of people who have the privilege of being able to try), it strikes me as the child of a generation (_that of our parents_) which witnessed the proliferation of a multitude of professional roles and fields of study that didn't even exist a few years prior. The worldview of a generation that had been taught to follow fairly linear tracks, and which, in a very short time, saw things take shape that they couldn't even imagine existing, was: _“do what you love and somehow you'll be able to market it."_ However, the evolution of this idea has completely removed the active component and it has become a rather short-sighted and limited reading of the world, split between _“do what you love and wait”_ and _“sacrifice yourself and do something you don't like but that will be useful.”_ You have to like the right things Is it possible to reach a synthesis? The “maybe” arises from observing _(certainly not an epochal discovery)_ that doing what you love is good advice if you like the right things: the ones the job market demands from you. Otherwise, you face the inevitable reckoning: having done something you like and not feeling valued in the very same world that told you to do what you love. If it is not universally applicable, and starting from the assumption that not everyone likes the same things and that individuals themselves experience an evolution in what they enjoy, is this a narrative that still makes sense to carry forward? I like to think the answer isn't a simple “no” _(which would presumably be resolved by merely changing direction from “do what you love” to “do what is useful”)_ but rather working toward a world that rediscovers the activity of choice and is ready to welcome those who find a way to adapt their skills and their education. Society should not tell you that you are useless , because that would be as limiting as telling you that somehow you will be useful; instead, it should give you the tools to ask yourself _(and possibly answer the question)_: if my experience has led me to be shaped this way, where do I fit in? This drive for action is omitted in most paths that churn out perfect profiles designed to fill spaces , but we have learned that, _in most cases_, this penalizes in the long run all those who witness the insertion of a new variable into their framework _(cf. the advent of AI)._ !Skills Matching! The advent of AI has changed many things, including the way we take couple photos. The Master's degree is useless Graduates in literary-humanistic disciplines in Italy see an employment rate of $\approx$ 62%, with no difference between Bachelor's and Master's graduates _(61.9% Master's and 62.6% Bachelor's, source: AlmaLaurea 2025)_ one year after graduation; not exactly a rosy outlook. If it is true that working is the way to confirm our place in the world _(something widely studied by those of us with a “humanistic” background!)_, along with the effort involved _(which should signify the will to be there)_, it is inevitable to conclude that digging through the "least worst" of compromises (also in light of the skills mismatch) has repercussions on the way we conceive ourselves in the world , on how we experience personal and professional satisfaction, and on staying satisfied and proud of the goals achieved and the education received. In conclusion, I believe that the thing that needs to be stimulated most of all is the active search for where my profile can fit and, _in the attempt to find an answer_, integrating and refining where necessary. The presence (and don't get me wrong... I recognize its importance) of figures who are increasingly trained in increasingly specific sectors penalizes those who have not followed a path with a very clear practical implication. Perhaps still a bit too much on the wave of illusion _(and maintaining a rather optimistic attitude)_, I believe that at the same time, this can be an advantage for those who, being more fluid, find more spaces in which to adapt. The fact remains that prioritizing something you are good at over something you like always works for those who like the right things (also because we usually become good at the things we like). What to do with this baggage of almost useless skills (in the strict sense of the term) is to try to read where there is truly a need for someone and try to become that person by building on what exists, but not remaining anchored solely to what we like. The initial _“maybe”_ therefore derives from the awareness that this is an optimistic vision of the world _(maybe in a year, flipping fries, I'll change my mind!)_, but one that has inevitably reckoned with the fact that what we like is not enough. !Skills Matching! Different passions also imply different lengths of stay in museums: there are those who lasted very little at the Louvre and those who would have stayed all day...

notes.title: 42195 meters later
notes.fullText: Sunday, April 12, two years after my last race, I ran my second marathon. I would like to say that standing on the starting line was the conclusion of a long journey of preparation and sacrifices that lasted months; the truth is that, _just over a month ago,_ encouraged by my uni's friends and hyped by the idea of running my "home" marathon , I decided to sign up at the last minute. Consequently, _despite already running about seventy km a week,_ I didn't have time to follow a "canonical" preparation: in the last 2 years, I have only run 14 high-intensity workouts and, in the last month of preparation, two long runs at marathon pace. Nonetheless, beyond being very satisfied with these 42.195 km , I think these moments were a period of great growth: during this time, in addition to university, work, and social life (split between Milan and Zurich) , an further source of stress was added that pushed me to make several reflections on the matter, listed below in no particular order. !Milano Zurich Marathon Milan Marathon, April 2024 - Zurich Marathon, April 2026 Dependent and Independent Variables Precisely estimating the final time of a marathon is very complex: besides not knowing all the factors that influence performance on race day, we cannot even quantify the impact of many of them. In short, defining a function that links input variables to the finish time is impossible. What is more reasonable to do, _at least in my experience_, is to focus on the variables under our control that have a greater marginal impact on the result. I cannot decide the temperature, the wind, or the humidity on the starting line; I cannot decide how many people will run at my pace, the unforeseen events along the route, or how much cheering I will receive along the 42 km. Similarly, I don't think it makes sense, _at least at amateur levels where all optimization problems must account for the constraints imposed by daily life,_ to focus on the grams of salt to take the night before, the weight of the racing gear, or other low-impact factors. On the contrary, the three levers I feel I have full control over, especially in the pre-race week, are, in order of importance: nutrition , training volume management , and sleep. Eat Pasta Run Fasta When I ran the Milan marathon two years ago, although I knew the guidelines on pre- and intra-workout carbohydrate intake, living with my family meant I didn't have full control over it. For this reason, _besides eating a bit more pasta al pesto the night before,_ I didn't optimize this aspect well. Since living alone, I've realized how much what I eat the day before drastically impacts performance. Consequently, measuring and controlling the grams of carbohydrates in the days before and during prolonged endurance activities immediately became a habit without great extra costs or effort. In the days preceding the Zurich marathon, I didn't change my total caloric intake much; rather, I reduced fats to a minimum in favor of carbohydrates _(without reducing fiber)_ and maintained a diet based on a few simple ingredients : vegetables, complex carbohydrates, very little added sugar, no fried or processed foods, and very little fruit. The night before the marathon, _after testing the same meal twice during training "long runs",_ I had dinner with 200g of rice, 400g of vegetables, 140g of oats, and 400g of white yogurt: approximately 290 grams of net carbohydrates 12 hours before the race. The next morning, following an already established routine, I had breakfast with 3 cereal bars of 25g, 5 tablespoons of sugar, and a cup of instant barley _(the only liquids of the morning)_, avoiding weighing down my stomach or overdoing caffeine and liquids. Finally, during the marathon, I took 8 gels of 25g of carbs each , for a total of about 70g of carbohydrates per hour spread over 2h 50'. I didn't bend over backward to optimize micronutrients or meal timing to the milligram; I preferred to follow a simple plan: very few sources, tracked carbohydrates, and consolidated routines. Even though my nutrition wasn't perfectly aligned with guidelines _(I think I can push higher with pre-race carbs),_ I had full control of the situation, aware of how my body would react. !Milano Zurich Marathon So much money spent on gels! Some of my favorites. Sleep is as beautiful as it is overrated Secondly, regarding volume management in the preceding weeks _(the famous tapering!)_, guidelines suggest cutting the total mileage by about 25% _(in the penultimate week)_ and then by 40-50% _(in the last)_, while maintaining a few intensity sessions to _"keep the legs turning."_ In my peak week, I ran 95 km; in the first taper week 64 km and, excluding the marathon itself, only 46 km in the seven days preceding the race; noticing, as the marathon approached, a very pleasant sense of lightness in my legs. I put sleep last because, compared to the other two levers, I believe it has a much more limited potential for positive impact. Getting enough sleep in the two or three preceding days is a necessary condition to feel rested, yet, from my experience, I am convinced that increasing the duration of rest beyond a certain threshold _(in my case 7-8 hours a night)_ does not correlate positively with performance. Regardless of individual data or what the guidelines suggest, I think the most important thing is to adopt an intentional and especially analytical approach, whose precision increases exponentially as it converges toward race day and the margin of error tends asymptotically to zero as you approach the starting line. !Milano Zurich Marathon Charts and data from the weeks before the race. An Imperfect Science From the previous paragraph, it clearly emerges how the marathon is a very imperfect science : many data points are at play, _both quantitative and qualitative,_ and their interpretation is almost never objective. I believe, in fact, that much more analytical skill is required than one might think, both to give meaning to the numbers we read on Strava or the Garmin app, and to learn to listen to one's body. As mentioned before, estimating, and therefore deciding in advance, your race pace is the result of a cross-analysis of previous workouts, fatigue levels, temperature, elevation, course layout, and many other factors we often don't control. Although it might seem easy, a tiny upward error is enough to ruin a race: starting even just a few seconds per kilometer too fast risks making the impact with "the wall" much more violent. The most you can do on race day is pure execution: leave as little as possible to chance and constantly monitor your status, checking your pace at short and regular intervals _(always use the manual lap, never rely on the watch's GPS!)_, keeping an eye on your heart rate and staying attuned to your feelings. Even in this case, it’s not rocket science: these are all habits accessible at zero cost even to us amateurs, without requiring any extra layer of complexity. The only allowed form of doping The marathon, however, is not just about numbers, data, and carbohydrates; all of that represents only the foundation for a much larger celebration . I like to divide the 42 km into a first part _(up to about the 30th-33rd km)_ of apparent calm and control, during which you run just below your threshold, enjoying the route, the music, and the cheering, trying to maintain as composed a form as possible without getting mentally drained. The goal of this first phase is to reach the 10 km mark ready for what everyone calls the "real" race. Arriving there still feeling energetic and ready to suffer in this final phase is the greatest privilege a runner can have : it is the proof that everything, _up to that point,_ went smoothly, and the moment when, _in a brutally simple way,_ everything starts to suck just a little bit more. I believe that one of the most beautiful sensations to experience in a marathon is the coexistence, _growing in intensity,_ of contrasting feelings in the final kilometers: on one hand, feeling the body still capable of pushing but aching in every single part ; on the other, visualizing the finish line in advance, _often too far in advance._ In my experience, this is also the moment when cheering becomes a source of exogenous doping : running through live music, hearing your name shouted by total strangers, reading witty signs, and high-fiving children, _besides causing a constant thrill of emotion,_ translates into a literal push for your legs. !NY Graph Find someone who waits 3h for you in the rain - The calm after the storm - Clear discomfort going down stairs Wrong incentives with positive results Beyond the unique emotion felt by those tackling every kilometer of the race, major European and world marathons have become mainstream cultural events that often involve the entire citizenship, public entities, and local businesses. Just as happens in most high-attendance sporting events, the Zurich marathon brought together on a Sunday morning _(despite Switzerland usually being a ghost nation on Sundays, with everything closed and no one around)_ countless families and friends cheering, many volunteers making it all possible, numerous food trucks along the route and, _no less importantly,_ many companies, mostly local, sponsoring the event in search of visibility. Obviously, behind all this mobilization often lie monetary incentives born of capitalism : companies primarily have an economic interest in being present; people, on the other hand, are quite happy to receive a Garmin-branded cookie, a Lindt chocolate, or mints from the cantonal bank as a gift, only to then spend 25 francs on a cheeseburger and leave their email and personal data in exchange for promotional offers. While I would like to declare myself against this exasperation of capitalism and preach a return to a more essential version of this sport, _where the only focus is on the pure athletic gesture,_ I won't deny that I don't mind this way of practicing sport at all, where running mixes with much more and becomes the occasion for a great party. !NY Graph Children of capitalism - the haul on race day We are becoming slower Starting from the premise that running is good for people, I am overall happy that this sport has become increasingly accessible and practiced by the masses , even if often at the expense of average athletic performance. Data from the New York Marathon, _probably the most historical and famous to the general public,_ is the perfect example of this trend. _From 1970 to 2024,_ the number of participants has increased drastically, going from fewer than 100 finishers in the first edition to 55,495 runners who crossed the finish line in 2024. The average performance, on the contrary, has worsened: the average finish time rose from 3h 26' in 1971 to 4h 48' for women and 4h 18' for men in 2024, demonstrating a great democratization of the event, making it accessible no longer just to a sporting elite, but to the mass population. _(One should, however, also consider the improvement in performance due to technological evolution and training methods, the analysis of which is postponed to an indefinite future)._ !NY Graph !NY Graph NYC Marathon | Performance Evolution over 53 editions Marathon runners like to spend Even from an economic point of view, the world of running has undergone massive growth. Sticking with the New York Marathon, in 2024 the event generated an economic impact of 692 million dollars for the city, marking a 139% increase compared to the 2019 edition. If we analyze the entire year, activities organized by the NYRR brought nearly 1 billion dollars into the local economy: a figure comparable to the combined impact of all home games of the New York Yankees and the New York Mets. This phenomenon is not limited to the "Big Apple": for cities hosting major marathons, these sporting events are true money-making machines capable of attracting people, capital, and consumption, proving that their reach extends, very easily, well beyond the pure sporting sphere. That said, I conclude with two very low-level thoughts: - I wish for you to dream, prepare , and run a marathon - I admit I missed the marathon prep: this spring I held back a bit... in autumn we do it again, even better! --- To learn more New York Road Runners Celebrates Record-Breaking Economic and Community Impact NYC Marathon - Performance Evolution History of the NYC Marathon The Marathon Industry Makes $5.2 Billion a Year. Correre è di Sinistra

notes.title: Pleased, assorbed and worried by AI
notes.fullText: Since 2022, interest in the term _“AI”_ has increased by 2500% _(Google Trend)_ but, even though this topic is very close to my heart, I have always preferred not to speak out about it, partly because I didn't feel I had much relevant to add, and partly because, both on LinkedIn and in the _*name of a casual newspaper*_, the signal-to-noise ratio is already high enough. Only recently, however, after a few interesting discussions and inputs, and also to try and pin down my opinion to see if and how it evolves over the coming ~~months~~ years, I thought it would be a good idea to write down my concerns and hopes regarding artificial intelligence. !Corporate AI investments One of many chats on the subject! (with two web legends!) Give me a lever long enough, and I shall move the world _(Archimede)_ Using AI means, at least in the activities I am involved in, leveraging one's time and effort ; in other words, AI is a tool for amplifying our output. Even though I sometimes miss banging my head against intricate but very low-level problems _(writing Python functions by hand, searching for hidden information, or wasting time solving equations or integrals)_, I am convinced that current AI models make projects accessible and feasible that were previously unthinkable. They don't just allow us to stop wasting time on low-level problems; they allow us to work at a higher level of abstraction , focusing more on design and the desired output. My concern in this regard is that, by having "almost infinite" computational resources available at a very low cost, we risk failing to perform a cost/benefit analysis of a project _(since the costs are almost zero)_. We end up starting or implementing things _"just for the sake of it"_ that aren't always useful or that have diminishing marginal utility (it often happens, for example, that we develop a prototype, an idea, or write something and only later ask ourselves how useful and relevant it actually is). The same perplexity applies to the recent trend of wanting to build "agents" for every activity: do we really need to automate everything? How often is this justified by a qualitatively superior output compared to a human one, and how often is it pure show-off or a reflection of mental laziness? Having a powerful tool at our disposal does not imply having to exploit it in every circumstance: I am convinced that, _in somes cases still_, using GenAI is an overkill and an unnecessary push dictated by the trend of the moment. With the necessary skills, _old-school_ purely algorithmic solutions or, _even "worse"_, purely human-based solutions are still qualitatively superior. !Corporate AI investments Thinking of a new field of application for AI? Someone has probably already thought of it. There's ai for that! If you concentrate and look closely, everything will clarify itself _(Haruki Murakami)_ My biggest fear is not that AI will take away our jobs, but rather that it will take away our ability to concentrate on a complex problem for long periods of time. Building something, solving structured problems, and writing elaborate texts are all activities that require time and mental effort _(which is why there isn't a text generated from scratch by AI on this blog yet)_. However, thanks to the use of more or less complex AI solutions _(from the free version of an LLM to expensive ad-hoc agents)_, all of this often translates into prompts of a few lines, written with imprecise grammar and syntax and without much reflection behind them, hoping that the model somehow "understands" our intentions and spits out something relevant. We lose the entire part of problem analysis and decomposition, the deep dive, and the associated effort, ending up—in most tasks—being satisfied with an instant output but settling for a mediocre result or, worse, one that doesn't belong to us or doesn't reflect the nature of the problem in detail. For this reason as well, I don't feel comfortable comparing a request to an LLM to a Google search: while the former is instantaneous and purely "passive," a search engine requires choosing, verifying, and analyzing every source. I don't think, however, that this is the only way to interact with AI. It is indeed still possible to complement _(and be helped by)_ your work with GPT, Gemini, or Grok: entering a state of deep work together with the model, having a clear macroscopic problem and breaking it down into smaller tasks to delegate, brainstorming, researching relevant information faster and, above all, constantly stress-testing your own convictions thanks to their enormous analytical capacity. I have noticed that, in many cases, the primary goal is no longer to obtain the highest quality output, but rather to maximize the result/effort ratio or, even worse, to minimize the effort : goals for which, _unfortunately_, LLMs are perfectly suited. Furthermore, in activities where the ends are path-dependent _(primarily studying)_, the use of AI often proves counterproductive . Although I don't want to seem opposed to AI in education _(and I use Gemini myself to help me study)_, in activities where real mental effort is required, I still try to use it as little as possible. There are tasks for which I am quite happy to save time _(translating and correcting emails, formatting texts, retrieving specific information online and much more)_ and activities where I am happy to have "sub-optimal" productivity for the sake of spending a few extra hours on them _(which is why I am grateful to have completed most of my education before GPT-2 in 2019 and why I am fully committed to taking notes "manually," even though Gemini could do it in a few seconds starting from my slides)._ Even though solving integrals, manually writing Python code, and extracting signal from complex texts are now activities easily delegated to an AI, I am convinced that the process through which one arrives at the result is highly formative and allows us to activate parts of our brain _(if necessary, I am ready to be proven wrong by neuroscience)_ that are fundamental for the development of problem-solving skills. AI is, in fact, removing from our lives, _in a progressive and often subtle way,_ all those occasions where mental effort and making choices are necessary. Even if in some cases this effort may be an end in itself without a direct positive outcome, I don't like the idea of a daily life where thinking about problems is no longer required , where we no longer have to run a thousand calculations to evaluate alternatives or iterate several times on the same solution before reaching a convincing result. !Corporate AI investments The list of my most recent discussions with Chat-GPT - One of my many Homework, theorems, and moldy cheese What worries me most about all this is the long-term impact on primary and secondary education. AI and education are traveling at speeds that are not even remotely comparable : while on one hand everw few weeks a new model is released, often more high-performing and capable of tackling long and complex problems, teaching methods have unfortunately lagged behind, caught unprepared by this revolution (_80% of US students report using AI at school, but only 50% of institutions have rules regarding it and only 6% of these rules are clear, source: Staford AI report _). In fact, I am convinced that, at least until the final years of high school, a frontier model _(2026)_ is capable of outperforming even the most brilliant students. The most obvious _(and disastrous)_ consequence is that every student is perfectly aware that any problem assigned at school is easily solvable in a few clicks and, potentially, every difficulty can be "bypassed" with a photo of the assignment and a short prompt. Added to this is the fact that no method is being taught to use these new tools, which, given their enormous potential, require at least a basic level of knowledge to be used effectively. However, I find that, _despite being very bearish regarding the short-term relationship between education and AI,_ it cannot be denied that in this field AI remains a revolutionary resource, at least as much as the invention of the printing press or Wikipedia. Indeed, I am amazed by how incredible it is to have access to (almost) unlimited knowledge instantaneously, in the ways I prefer and at all desired levels of "superficiality" or depth _(from asking to explain the proof of a mathematical theorem step-by-step to asking what to do when Parmigiano is moldy)._ !Corporate AI investments Lecture and exam: where is AI? Zurigo has more AI-agents than inhabitants Although this resource is, _at least for me,_ accessible at zero cost and effort, I am well aware that I am in a bubble. For a few months now I have been living in Zurigo, the city with the highest AI-talent density but, regardless of that, I spend my days in a very young, favorable context accustomed to technological progress, and I happily work in company that _(despite being as far as possible from a startup run by twenty-year-olds in t-shirts and Patagonia)_ is proudly pushing hard on the adoption of these tools. I am aware, however, that the world travels on very different tracks: even among people of my generation, the use and knowledge of LLMs remain very limited, adoption in educational environments is often not even considered _(and not even accepted)_, and not all companies are looking for ways to best exploit this technology. Moreover, its spread strictly depends on the reference sector: software engineering has certainly been the most revolutionized field and, _in general,_ all STEM subjects have strongly felt the positive effects. Not all "intellectual" jobs, however _(clearly leaving aside bricklayers and chefs who, at least for the moment, are safe!)_, are affected in the same way and, consequently, those working in those fields are much less involved and touched by this revolution. !Corporate AI investments Grateful to have taken the Introduction of Computer Science exam in front of a piece of paper, "translating" binary numbers and writing C code, rather than "messaging" with Claude! OpenAI is still a non-profit My final concern, however, regards another bubble: the one linked to the economic and financial nature behind this trend. I do not question the capabilities of Claude Opus 4.7 or GPT-5; rather, I remain skeptical about how economically sustainable all this is, both for the companies behind the large models and for the third-party companies investing in their adoption and, _ultimately_, for us final consumers given that, at the moment, this entire great revolution is based on companies that are leaking from all sides. OpenAI , despite its 900 million users _(of which only 5% are paying!)_, plans 14 billion dollar losses _(Yahoo Finance)_ and, again according to estimates, increasing its liabilities until 2029. The forecasts for Anthropic , more oriented toward an enterprise model than B2C, are slightly better _(with a positive cash flow predicted as early as 2028)_ but still, at least for now, they remain in the red. A similar game is being played by xAI _(Musk's company behind Grok)_ which, according to analysts, burns more tham 1 billion per quarter. _As Gemini itself suggests to me when I ask it to extract some "bad taste" jokes from this situation_, Gen-AI thus represents the greatest socialism experiment in Silicon Valley: the rich put up the money and the people use the models to avoid reading books and to work less. !Corporate AI investments source: Stanford Ai Report 2026 - Economy Jokes aside, the mechanisms of circular financing between tech providers and AI scale-ups represent another cause for concern. The pattern, _now validated multiple times by various Big Tech companies,_ involves a hardware/software infrastructure provider (read NVIDIA for graphics cards, Microsoft for Cloud infrastructure) providing capital to an external company (read OpenAI) which, with those same funds, buys services or products from its own financier, inflating financial and growth metrics on both sides. Despite all these cracks, growth expectations, investments in Private Equity and Credit (markets that are anything but transparent and liquid—another potentially alarming characteristic!) toward these entities and, above all, the valuations of chip providers (NVIDIA first and foremost, the "shovel seller" in the gold rush) are at all-time highs. All this promises nothing but a brilliant future , yet outlines expectations very different from the current reality and actual cash flows. On the other hand, I wonder how much final users are actually willing to pay for a premium subscription: most people do not need Claude Code or Gemini Pro and, consequently , are not willing to pay dozens of dollars a month. At the same time, however, the models accessible for free maintain a significant cost, both for one-off development and for maintenance, which, sooner or later , someone will have to bear. That said, I don't think (and I don't hope) that the AI bubble will burst as violently as it did during the dot-com crisis in the early 2000s (famous last words!) ; I believe (and perhaps hope!) that this phenomenon will downsize, both in terms of hype and in purely economic terms, eventually leading us to "gently" adjust to this new daily reality. So, whether we like it or not, we better learn to live with it! !Corporate AI investments Most of the things I like to do are still AI-free! To learn more AI 2027: a dystopian story Stanford AI report 2026 The Open AI Bubble: How Big Tech May Be Inflating Revenue Perché stiamo usando l'AI in modo sbagliato (per ora) Cal Newport: In Defense of Thinking

notes.title: Should you go leveraged?
notes.fullText: Disclaimer: this is not financial advice. Wall Street is the only place that people ride to in a Rolls-Royce to get advice from those who take the subway _(W. Buffet)_ Lesson 0 of personal finance teaches that stocks are risky and bonds are safe. A few chapters later, you learn the importance of diversification: _if a stock goes down, the bond usually goes up._ By Lesson 6 or 7, depending on your personal finance guru of choice, it is explained that, _in the long run,_ the stock market tends to rise. The truth is that reality is far more complex: personal finance and consequent financial planning are strictly personal and time-dependent optimization problems . There is no "one-size-fits-all" strategy: rather, there are solutions that adapt to each individual's present and future goals. It is, in fact, reasonable to think that today, _at 23 years old,_ I have very different perspectives and needs than those I will have in 10, 30 _or... 100 years;_ which is why it is equally logical that my risk tolerance _(and consequently my exposure to related factors)_ evolves over time. !Vanguard Total World ETF This Great Love for the USA Generally, there are two main ways to make a portfolio more "aggressive" : 1. Increasing concentration , by allocating a larger portion of capital into risky assets (traditionally *equity*). 2. Using financial leverage , by borrowing capital to amplify exposure. Most retail investors tend to focus on the first option _(perceived as more conventional),_ often overestimating the real level of geographical and sector diversification of their investments. At the same time, they tend to associate leverage with something too dangerous and speculative , preferring concentration because it is "psychologically" more reassuring than debt. !Vanguard Total World ETF What I mean when I say the USA is the engine of the world... However, there are at least two technical reasons why leverage deserves a closer look: 1. The share allocatable to risky assets has a physical limit _(100%)._ Beyond this threshold, the only way to increase concentration is through stock-picking , which however exposes one to specific _(idiosyncratic)_ risk that is not rewarded by the market. 2. For the same level of overall risk _(intended both as volatility and the probability of not reaching one's financial goals)_, using leverage on a well-diversified portfolio can lead to preferable results. But "using leverage" means everything and nothing, as it is an extremely broad concept that depends on numerous variables: the leverage ratio, the instruments used, deleveraging strategies, rebalancing frequency and the nature of the underlying assets. For the retail investor, the most viable paths are usually two: margin investing or using Leveraged ETFs , financial instruments that amplify the daily return _(or other timeframes)_ of the underlying by a constant factor through periodic internal rebalancing. Note, however, that the market for these instruments _(especially in Europe)_ is still relatively small, and product availability varies significantly based on tax residence. !Occupy Wall Street One of the most iconic photos from the Occupy Wall Street peaceful protest movement of 2011. The world economy is today a giant casino _(Fidel Castro)_ The following analysis therefore focuses on passive investment strategies with variable leverage ratios applied to the daily movements of the index. To do this, I simulated the creation of "synthetic" leveraged ETFs based on major world indices, applying the following formula to calculate their return: $$ R_{L,t} = L \cdot R_{i,t} - \left[ \frac{TER}{252} + (L-1) \cdot \frac{Rate_t + Spread}{252} \right] $$ Where $L$ is the leverage ratio, $R_{i,t}$ is the daily return of the underlying index/ETF, $TER$ is the *Total Expense Ratio* of the ETF, and $Rate_t + Spread$ quantifies the cost of financing for the borrowed capital. Instead of validating the portfolios through a simple historical backtest _(often full of bias)_, an approach based on Monte Carlo simulations via the Bootstrap method was adopted, sampling returns from the period _(unfortunately limited by data availability)_ 2012-2026. The results remain distorted and influenced by this choice, characterized by a regime of negative or very low real rates for most of the time and an unprecedented US equity dominance . Consequently, it would be appropriate to replicate the same analysis by removing any survivorship bias in the composition of the indices and, _even more importantly_, comparing different strategies during periods of high rates, stagflation, or prolonged recessions. Furthermore, the analyzed portfolios are a narrow subset of those possible; I have in fact focused on the most common ones in the retail world using the dollar as the reference currency _(for greater data availability, thus omitting exchange rate risk)_ and assuming transaction costs and capital gains taxes flat at 26% _(regardless of the asset class)_. This does not change the fact that, by _adapting the tickers,_ the analysis can be extended to any portfolio and currency. !Results Montecarlo Montecarlo Spaghetti As mentioned, since personal finance is an "imperfect and subjective science" , we must first define the metrics to optimize. Having a time horizon in the range of decades, a large human capital considered "safe" _(which is why, overall, I am under-exposed to equities)_ and the ability to accept large swings, optimizing CAGR while keeping the Max Drawdown within acceptable limits appears to be a rational choice. Since these are simulations, it is appropriate to understand how to interpret the distribution of results: for most metrics, I preferred the median value to reduce the impact of outliers, while still keeping an eye on the most disastrous cases ($p95$). !Results Montecarlo Results of the 10,000 Monte Carlo simulations with annual rebalancing and 26% capital gains tax Comparing the results, it is no surprise to see how 100% equity (VT) , in the spot _(1x)_ and leveraged _(1.5x, 2x, 3x)_ versions, shows increasing median CAGRs and Max Drawdowns: respectively 13%, 17%, 21%, 27% and -27%, -41%, -52% and -69% . The _(daily)_ leverage factor, however, does not reflect linearly on the _(annual)_ compound returns due to financing costs and, above all, the volatility drag , the impact of which is well approximated by the following: $$ g_L \approx L \cdot \mu - \frac{1}{2} L^2 \sigma^2 $$ The basic optimization problem of leveraged investing consists of maximizing the expected return $g_L$ as a function of the expected arithmetic return and the variance of the underlying. Note, however, that in sideways markets, _where $\mu$ tends to zero,_ the term $-\frac{1}{2} L^2 \sigma^2$ keeps the position in the red. !volatility drag More importantly: as leverage increases, risk-adjusted efficiency worsens: the Sharpe and Sortino ratios progressively decrease and the risk of margin call increases. For the most risk-averse, it should also be noted that 100% stocks 3x , in the worst case _($p5$)_, presents a CAGR of -2% , which implies a negative nominal return in the long run. In parallel, it emerges how targeted leverage can improve a balanced portfolio. In the "vanilla" version, the 60/40 has a median CAGR of 8% , with contained volatility _(0.10 vs 0.17 for 100% stocks)_ and a median Max Drawdown of just 17%. By applying a 2x leverage factor to the equity component only , a median CAGR of 13% is obtained _(identical to spot 100% equity),_ but with a potentially more favorable distribution of results and different risk management _(especially in terms of goal-based investing, having 40% in government bonds capable of meeting planned expenses or emergencies)._ !Results Montecarlo All That Glitters Is Not Gold It is curious to note how applying leverage also to the bond portion does not bring the hoped-for benefits: the portfolio in question is in fact Pareto-inferior compared to the equity-only leverage version due to the ratio between bond yield and debt cost: if the underlying return does not significantly exceed the cost of money and the volatility drag, leverage destroys value instead of creating it. Finally, the All Weather and Golden Butterfly confirm themselves as the most conservative, with reduced CAGRs but extremely contained drawdowns in the spot variants. These allocations suffer from the fact that, _over the last decade,_ US equities have outperformed almost every other asset class. However, if we consider the Ulcer Index _(which synthesizes well the depth and duration of periods of suffering),_ the Golden Butterfly and All Weather rank at the top, maintaining a good risk profile even in the leveraged versions. In conclusion, using leverage does not necessarily mean chasing unrealistic returns at the expense of stability and, consequently, should not be seen only as a speculative tool reserved for _Long Term Capital Management_, but rather as an additional degree of freedom in asset allocation to overcome the limits of traditional diversification and optimize capital efficiency. That said, far be it from me to propose investment strategies or "lecture" on leverage _(for that, The Italian Leather Sofa already exists);_ this analysis, _with all its inevitable simplifications_, is for entertainment and informational purposes only. I refer you to the repo github for the curious. Cheers! !Vanguard Total World ETF The important thing is not to end up like LTCM... --- Per saperne di più Leveraged ETFs: la repo GitHub (made with ❤️ by me) Leveraged ETF Investing _(Tal Miller, 2021)_ Long-Term Investing With Leveraged Exchange Traded Funds _(Loehr et Lamb, 2013)_ Why Do Most Investors Choose Concentration Over Leverage? _(ARQ, 2012)_ Investing with Leverage _(Ben Felix, 2019)_ Parliamo di Leva e DCA _(ITA - Mr. Rip, 2023)_ Gli ETF a Leva: analisi dei veri costi e rendimenti _(ITA - Coletti, 2024)_ The Italian Leather Sofa _(Blog, Nicola Protasoni)_

notes.slug: personal-finance/Alternative Thinking Why Do Most Investors Choose Concentration Over Leverage
notes.title: Alternative Thinking Why Do Most Investors Choose Concentration Over Leverage
notes.fullText: Alternative Thinking Why Do Most Investors Choose Concentration Over Leverage? Academics working within the mean-variance framework showed over 50 years ago that the latter approach leads to higher expected returns. Why would investors concentrate in one dominant risk when it has not offered a similarly dominant reward? We have to seek answers from outside the mean-variance framework. Why Do Most Investors Choose Concentration Over Leverage? Executive Summary C In recent decades, institutional investors have migrated toward a 60/40 stock/bond allocation, which is diversified by capital, but concentrated in equity risk. - Even the major competitors to the by-now traditional 60/40 portfolio (including the “Yale Model” and the “Canada Model”) share this risk concentration. - Theoretically and empirically, we find leverage risk has been better compensated than concentration risk. Moreover, we believe that leverage risk can be more manageable than concentration risk. - Exchanging some concentration risk for leverage risk is not for everyone, which is one reason we expect a return premium for investors who pursue it. Two Paths Investors seeking higher returns have two broad options: they can concentrate their portfolio in assets or asset classes with the highest expected returns, or they can diversify (ideally holding the portfolio of risky assets with the highest Sharpe ratio (SR)) and then apply leverage to reach their desired level of portfolio volatility. Academics working within the mean-variance framework showed over 50 years ago that the latter approach leads to higher expected returns – see Exhibit 1, which uses the simplified example of two risky assets, stocks and bonds. 1 Portfolios moving along the blue line sell bonds to buy more stocks, and portfolios along the green line sell cash to buy both stocks and bonds. Each approach makes the portfolio riskier, but the green approach is more rewarding over the long-term. Which One Do Investors Choose? In recent decades, institutional investors have migrated toward a 60/40 stock/bond allocation 2 (as evidenced by typical institutional holdings and perhaps market-cap weights) while diversifying increasingly globally and making small allocations to other asset classes. A 60/40 portfolio may appear diversified, but its risk emanates almost exclusively from the more volatile asset class, stocks. The correlation between the monthly returns of a global 60/40 portfolio and a global equity index is 0.99 (1990-2011). Exhibit 1 | Expected Returns vs Risk (Volatility) – The Classical Picture ![](images/220a5c800a34879e9fd5d7ff982bc7ebe6b55ec2ed299439b488890e92336034.jpg) line | Risk | Expected Return | | --- | --- | | Risk-Free Rate | ~0.5 | | 100% Bonds | ~0.5 | | 60% / 40% Stocks/Bonds | ~0.7 | | 100% Stocks | ~0.9 | Source: AQR Exhibit 2 shows some of the major competitors to the by-now traditional 60/40 investment model, the “Yale Model” (or “Endowment Model”) and the “Canada Model,” both of which invest heavily in alternative asset classes (hedge funds, private equity, real estate/infrastructure, natural resources/commodities, etc.) and expect to reap illiquidity premiums and better perceived alpha opportunities from private assets. 3 However, these alternative assets contain such high equity market betas that these portfolios are still highly exposed to directional equity market moves. Over the past decade, the correlation between the quarterly returns of a composite alternative asset portfolio and a global equity index is 0.75 (per asset class: 0.86 for hedge funds, 0.80 for private equity, 0.45 for commodities and 0.21 for real estate). 4 All three major investment models thus choose concentration and avoid direct leverage, even while embracing embedded leverage. 5 Investors that actively exploit and lever the superior Sharpe ratios (SR) of low-risk investments are a distinct minority. They include many LBO managers, quantitative investors, and also Warren Buffett. The Sage of Omaha does not profess to be a fan of diversification, but he has favored low-beta stocks as much as value stocks and uses leverage through insurance company float (the difference between insurance premium payments and much later compensation payments). 6 Exhibit 2 | Summary of Major Approaches to Building Portfolios ![](images/5ea5c583cc7401a433e6f4fd2e94629ec33ec3b25ef4267e05f97bb332b3b683.jpg) flowchart ```mermaid graph TD A["Equity Premium Reliant Investment Models"] --> B["Traditional Liquid Market Focus"] A --> C["More Emphasis on Alternatives, Illiquids"] A --> D["Diversification Across Premiums (Market & Alternative Betas)"] B --> E["External Mgmt Tilt"] B --> F["In-house Mgmt Tilt"] B --> G["60/40 Model"] B --> H["Norway Model"] C --> I["External Mgmt Tilt"] C --> J["Endowment Model"] C --> K["Canada Model"] D -.-> L["A Better Starting Point?"] ``` Source: AQR. Data description: The 60/40 portfolio consists of 60% MSCI World (developed equity markets) index, 20% Barclays U.S. Aggregate fixed-income index, 20% Citigroup World Government Bond Index ex-U.S., currency-hedged. The Alternatives-4 is a composite of direct real estate (NCREIF transaction-based index), commodity futures (SP GSCI index), hedge funds (DJ CS index), and private equity (Cambridge Associates private equity index). To give the four constituents roughly equal long-run volatilities, the nominal weight of real estate is 28%, hedge funds 38%, commodities 14%, and private equity 20%. The last-quarter observations for real estate and private equity are not yet available; beta-based proxies are used instead. What Explains Investor Preference for Concentrated Equity Risk? In a mean-variance framework, the only reason investors would concentrate in equities is if equities offered a uniquely high long-run SR, to offset their disproportionately high risk compared to other asset classes. 7 However, empirically, major asset classes have delivered broadly similar long-run SRs. For example, between 1971 and 2010, global equities, U.S. Treasuries, and commodities all had SRs between 0.24 and 0.29. 8 And there is widespread evidence of low-risk investments offering relatively high SRs as well as evidence of attractive long-run SRs from long-short strategies focused on low-risk investing. Why would investors concentrate in one dominant risk when it has not offered a similarly dominant reward? (Note that higher long-run return is not enough; a higher long-run SR is needed to explain this puzzle within the CAPM.) We have to seek answers from outside the mean-variance framework. It turns out the equity premium has several “advantages” over other ways of raising long-run returns (including value investing, levered diversification, illiquid assets, market timing, and insurance selling): - Confidence: due to standard financial theories (CAPM and multi-factor models, or participating in economic growth) and the most extensive empirical evidence, including a positive equity premium in all 19 countries with history since 1900. 9 - Familiarity: due to minimal peer risk or maverick risk. Recall the Keynes quote of failing conventionally; relatively few money managers lost their jobs when their portfolios lost fortunes in the tech bust and recent financial crisis. But, failing when others are all succeeding, even if on the path to long-term better success, is not always a recipe for career advancement. - Ancillary benefits: including deep capacity (the bottleneck for many other approaches), relatively low costs, high liquidity, and embedded leverage. - Leverage aversion: avoided because it has the “feel” of speculation, while concentration is anchored in conventionality. This bias is based on mistaken beliefs but no doubt contributes to the preference for concentration. A better reason for investors’ leverage aversion is the real risk of being forced to delever in bad times, discussed below. This list consists mainly of real-world descriptive facts and/or excuses for suboptimal investment behavior. 10 They do not make concentrated equity market exposure a better long-run investment, except perhaps for one reason: they may enable better time consistency. Investors are more likely to succumb to doubts and "throw in the towel" after 2-3 bad years when they rely on other return sources. In contrast, when relying on the equity premium, investors may forgive even a bad decade. The arguments above may give investors the patience to maintain their supposedly long-run positions through a bad patch. Leverage Risk Is Well-Rewarded But Needs To Be Managed The benefits of diversification are well-known. A better balanced portfolio will have lower volatility and likely a higher SR than a concentrated portfolio, unless the high-risk investments offer a commensurately high SR. Investors can then use leverage (or invest in the riskless asset) to achieve the acceptable risk level for their well-diversified portfolio. A growing empirical literature suggests analogous patterns in many different contexts: lower-risk investments offer higher long-run SRs than their speculative peers. The empirical reward for risktaking is not what most investors expect. Within every asset class, bearing small amount of risk appears amply rewarded, while further risk-taking and especially moving to become concentrated in the most speculative investments within an asset class is poorly rewarded or even punished (see Exhibit 3). 11 Thus, leverage risk has been better compensated than concentration risk. Moreover, we believe that leverage risk can be more manageable than concentration risk. If investors let equity market direction dominate their portfolio performance, they are doomed to follow the rollercoaster ride of market gyrations with little recourse. Leverage is a risk that must – and can – be managed. The ultimate risk is being forced to delever with the crowd at firesale prices. Still, managing leverage risk (which includes keeping large cash balances, limiting illiquidity, establishing caps on exposures, and monitoring counterparties) is arguably easier than managing concentration risk (effectively, market-timing). Investors should also be nuanced about different types of leverage (e.g., levering up liquid investments with low standalone volatility, such as 2-year Treasury futures, is less risky than levering up illiquid, high-volatility ventures). This approach is not for everyone, which is one reason we expect a return premium for investors who pursue it. Like many other active approaches, low-risk investing relies on someone else being “on the other side.” 12 We do not expect the majority of investors to become more pro-leverage so soon after 2008, both due to regulatory changes and investors’ own preferences, which leaves better reward for risk for the minority who can exploit these opportunities. Exhibit 3 | Within Asset Classes, Lower Risk Securities Are More Efficient, 1926‒2011 ![](images/4dc9154fa115791c9706ebcdd860f3b5d903041f82f7ce589ef4e2a9a461fa94.jpg) bar | Category | Sharpe Ratio (1926-2011) | | --- | --- | | Value-Wtd Treas | ~0.48 | | Value-Wtd Stocks | ~0.39 | | 5yr Treasuries | ~0.43 | | >9yr Treasuries | ~0.29 | | 20yr Treasuries | ~0.28 | | 1) Lowest Beta | ~0.67 | | 2) | ~0.55 | | 3) | ~0.53 | | 4) | ~0.52 | | 5) | ~0.49 | | 6) | ~0.46 | | 7) | ~0.39 | | 8) | ~0.35 | | 9) | ~0.29 | | 10) Highest Beta | ~0.25 | Sharpe ratios on U.S. assets between 1926 and 2011. Value Weighted Treasuries and Stocks are CRSP data. The 5-year and the 20-year Treasury are Ibbotson Associates SBBI Intermediate Term and Long Term Government Bond Indices from Morningstar; they chain single bonds with roughly 5-year and 20-year maturities. The >9-year index is a valueweighted portfolio of all Treasuries with maturities greater than 9 years, from CRSP. The CRSP value-weighted Treasury index (the first column) includes also money market assets and thus has a shorter average maturity than the other Treasury portfolios. The beta-sorted stock portfolios are based on CRSP data with betas calculated using daily data over the past year. Broad-based securities indices are unmanaged and are not subject to fees and expenses typically associated with managed accounts or investment funds. Past performance is not a guarantee of future performance. An Alternative to Equities as Sole Drivers of Returns We maintain that aggressive risk-balanced diversification among well-chosen return sources is the most reliable way to achieve long-run investment success. We believe investors are more likely to achieve CPI+5% if they embrace a modest amount of innovation, particularly in diversification. Leverage is one tool that helps (some) investors diversify and avoid the trap of equity concentration. In a long-only context, investors should consider risk parity investing across asset classes, and scaling up low-volatility securities (and reducing exposure to high volatility securities) within asset classes. Long-short strategies should be explicitly managed to provide uncorrelated returns. Hedge fund risk premiums represent a liquid approach, and reinsurance ‒ a strategy investors are increasingly considering ‒ represents an illiquid return source. Finally, the combination counts: a portfolio of return sources should be balanced so that each can meaningfully contribute to risk and return.

notes.title: La Matematica Del Benessere Toia
notes.fullText: La matematica del benessere Derivata del benessere Processi con memoria Il trucco della mente La trappola della stabilità Il paradosso dell'ambizione Epilogo C'è stato un momento della mia vita in cui mi sono accorto di non essere né felice né infelice. Semplicemente… ero. Non esaltato dai momenti belli, né schiacciato da quelli bruti. E lì ho capito qualcosa: la felicità non è uno stato, è una variazione. È una derivata. Forse essere felici non significa sentirsi bene, ma sentirsi meglio di prima. Non conta dove sei. Conta da dove vieni. Una variazione positiva in uno stato mediocre può darti una gioia improvvisa. Una condizione perfetta, ma piatta nel tempo, ti lascia vuoto. Derivata del benessere $$ \text{Felicità} (t, \omega) = \frac{\partial}{\partial t} [ \text{Benessere} (t) ] + \epsilon (t, \omega) $$ Il cervello sembra agire come un operatore differenziale, ma non solo. Essenzialmente il concetto è questo: se il benessere non cresce, la felicità è 0. Più velocemente cresce, più felice sei. Ma. Ma a questo va aggiunto un termine di errore, che dipende dal tempo, dalla nostra storia passata, e onestamente da qualcos'altro che non saprei nemmeno come formalizzare. Quel termine è il nostro "rumore emotivo": dipende dal tempo e dalla nostra storia passata. E quando la derivata si annulla – anche se sei "in alto" – resta solo quel termine di errore a guidare la percezione del benessere. Essenzialmente, aggiunge complessità. Come se non bastasse quella che c'era già. È l'adattamento edonico. La curva si stabilizza. La derivata diventa 0. E tu ti chiedi: "Perché non sento nulla?" Iniziamo a sentirci vuoti, impazienti, come se mancasse qualcosa. Anche quando, razionalmente, sappiamo di stare bene. È questo il cuore dell'adattamento edonico: ci si abitua a tutto, anche alla serenità. Processi con memoria C'è una cosa che ho capito col tempo, e forse è il motivo per cui non riesco mai a godermi davvero un momento bello, almeno non a lungo. Non viviamo mai solo nel momento presente. Anche quando tutto è perfetto, c'è una parte della mente che si confronta con ciò che è successo ieri, la settimana scorsa, o tre mesi fa. E se non c'è un miglioramento percepibile… allora anche il "perfetto" inizia a svuotarsi. È come se il nostro cervello fosse una macchina che fa la media del passato, una media strana, con qualche peso in più per i momenti più recenti, ma anche qualche peso in più per alcuni periodi particolari. Un algoritmo implicito, che aggiorna continuamente il nostro stato emotivo. S(t) = f (α 0 ω 0 , α 1 ω 1 , … , α t−1 ω t−1 ) Dove ogni α i rappresenta un evento passato, e ogni ω i è il peso che gli diamo. Se fosse una media semplice, eventi più lontani peserebbero sempre meno. Ma non funziona. Perché ci sono periodi – alcuni giorni, alcune estati, certe settimane strane – che restano lì. Alcuni α i sono enormi, quella estate di tre anni fa? Quella settimana strana al liceo? Quel pomeriggio che non dovrebbe significare nulla? Hanno coefficienti inspiegabilmente alti. Altri α i sono quasi zero. Settimane intere della tua vita che pesano meno di un singolo momento. È come se il cervello avesse un algoritmo di pesatura completamente irrazionale. E il nostro livello di felicità? Dipende da quanto ci aspettiamo che questo stato migliorerà: $$ \text{Felicità} (t) \approx \mathbb{E} \left[ \frac{\partial S}{\partial t} \mid \alpha_{i} \omega_{i} \right] $$ In parole umane: siamo felici quando ci aspettiamo che le cose migliorino, o quando migliorano davvero rispetto al contesto da cui proveniamo. Ma quei periodi con α i alti... erano davvero così belli? Voglio dire: quei periodi che ricordo come estremamente piacevoli, produttivi... lo erano? Se ci ripenso, spesso non me ne rendevo conto mentre li vivevo. Non dicevo: "questo sarà un momento importante" allora. Anzi. A volte mi sentivo vuoto anche allora. Eppure la memoria li ha salvati. Li ha resi qualcosa. Ha preso una traiettoria ordinaria e l'ha piegata, scolpendola nel tempo. Perché succede? Non lo so. Ma trovo un piacere enorme nel cercare di capirlo. Nel provare a mettere ordine in una funzione che, forse, non sarà mai derivabile in modo elegante. Il trucco della mente Ma la mente ha un trucco. Un hack. Riflettere. Osservarsi da fuori. È come se potessimo uscire dal "grafico della felicità" e guardarlo. "Ah, ecco cosa sta succedendo. Il mio cervello sta cercando variazioni che non ci sono.
